what: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
text: Fits binomial regression model specific time point presence right-censored data competing risks. function implements Inverse Probability Censoring Weighted (IPCW) estimating equation approach.

title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
code: binreg(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   time = NULL,   beta = NULL,   type = c("II", "I"),   offset = NULL,   weights = NULL,   cens.weights = NULL,   cens.model = ~+1,   se = TRUE,   kaplan.meier = TRUE,   cens.code = 0,   no.opt = FALSE,   method = "nr",   augmentation = NULL,   outcome = c("cif", "rmst", "rmtl"),   model = c("default", "logit", "exp", "lin"),   Ydirect = NULL,   ... )

title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
text: formula formula object specifying outcome covariates. outcome must Event object (Event(time, cause)). data data frame containing variables formula. cause Numeric vector scalar indicating cause interest competing risks. time Numeric scalar indicating time point interest cumulative incidence. beta Optional numeric vector starting values coefficients. Defaults zeros. type Character string. Either "" (classic binomial regression) "II" (adds augmentation term). offset Optional numeric vector offsets linear predictor. weights Optional numeric vector weights score equations. cens.weights Optional numeric vector pre-calculated censoring weights. NULL, estimated internally. cens.model formula specifying censoring model. Defaults ~+1. Can include strata (e.g., ~strata(group)). se Logical. TRUE, computes standard errors based IPCW influence functions. kaplan.meier Logical. TRUE, uses Kaplan-Meier estimator IPCW weights; otherwise uses exponential baseline. cens.code Numeric code representing censored observations status variable. Defaults 0. .opt Logical. TRUE, optimization skipped starting values used. method Character string. Optimization method: "nr" (Newton-Raphson) "nlm". augmentation Optional numeric vector additional augmentation terms. outcome Character string. Outcome type: "cif" (Cumulative Incidence Function), "rmst" (Restricted Mean Survival Time), "rmtl" (Restricted Mean Time Lost). model Character string. Link function: "default" (auto-selects based outcome), "logit", "exp", "lin" (identity). Ydirect Optional numeric vector. provided, outcome used instead constructing one outcome. Useful custom IPCW adjustments. ... Additional arguments passed lower-level optimization functions.

title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
text: object class "binreg" containing coefficients, variance-covariance matrix,   influence functions (iid), model details.

title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
text: model estimates probability: $$P(T \leq t, \epsilon=1 | X ) = \text{expit}( X^T \beta) $$ Based binomial regresion IPCW response estimating equation: $$ X ( \Delta^{ipcw}(t) (T \leq t, \epsilon=1 ) - expit( X^T beta)) = 0 $$ $$\Delta^{ipcw}(t) = ((min(t,T)< C)/G_c(min(t,T)-)$$ IPCW adjustment response $$Y(t)= (T \leq t, \epsilon=1 )$$. Two types estimators available: type="": Solves standard IPCW estimating equation. type="II": Adds censoring augmentation term efficiency gains,   solving $$X \int E(Y(t)| T>s)/G_c(s) d \hat M_c$$. Alternatively, logitIPCW performs standard logistic regression IPCW weights applied directly likelihood. Thus solving $$ X  (min(T_i,t) < G_i)/G_c(min(T_i ,t)) ( (T \leq t, \epsilon=1 ) - expit( X^T beta)) = 0 $$ standard logistic regression IPCW weights. variance estimation based squared influence functions, options naive variance (assuming known censoring) robust variance (accounting censoring model estimation). Censoring model may depend strata (cens.model=~strata(gX)).

title: Binomial Regression for Censored Competing Risks Data — binreg
text: Blanche PF, Holt , Scheike T (2022). "logistic regression right censored data, without competing risks, use estimating treatment effects." Lifetime data analysis, 29, 441–482. Scheike TH, Zhang MJ, Gerds TA (2008). "Predicting cumulative incidence probability direct binomial regression." Biometrika, 95(1), 205–220.

title: Average Treatment Effect for Censored Competing Risks Data using Binomial Regression — binregATE
text: formula formula object specifying outcome covariates (see coxph). first covariate treatment variable coded factor. data data frame containing variables formula. cause Numeric scalar indicating cause interest competing risks. time Numeric scalar indicating time point interest. beta Optional numeric vector starting values coefficients. treat.model formula specifying logistic treatment model given covariates (e.g., treatment ~ covariate1 + covariate2). cens.model formula specifying censoring model. stratified Cox models without covariates supported (e.g., ~ strata(group)). offset Optional numeric vector offsets partial likelihood. weights Optional numeric vector weights score equations. cens.weights Optional numeric vector pre-calculated censoring weights. NULL, weights estimated internally. se Logical. TRUE, computes standard errors IPCW adjustment. FALSE, assumes IPCW weights known. type Character string. Either "" (classic binomial regression) "II" (adds augmentation term efficiency). kaplan.meier Logical. TRUE, uses Kaplan-Meier IPCW weights; otherwise uses \(\exp(-\text{Baseline})\). cens.code Numeric code representing censored observations status variable. .opt Logical. TRUE, optimization skipped starting values used. method Character string. Optimization method: "nr" (Newton-Raphson) "nlm". augmentation Optional numeric vector augmenting binomial regression. outcome Character string. Outcome type: "cif" (Cumulative Incidence Function, \(F(t|X)\)), "rmst" (Restricted Mean Survival Time, \(E(\min(T, t) | X)\)), "rmtl" (Restricted Mean Time Lost, \(E((\epsilon=\text{cause})(t - \min(T,t)) | X)\)). model Character string. Link function outcome model: "exp" "lin" (identity). "cif", "logit" typically used. Ydirect Optional numeric vector. Use outcome Y IPCW version instead constructing one outcome. typeATE Character string. Either "II" (censoring augmentation estimating equation) "" (standard). ... Additional arguments passed lower-level functions (e.g., binreg fits outcome model).

title: Average Treatment Effect for Censored Competing Risks Data using Binomial Regression — binregATE
text: object class c("binreg", "ATE") containing: coef Estimated coefficients outcome model. riskDR Double-robust marginal risk estimates treatment level. riskG G-formula marginal risk estimates treatment level. difriskDR Difference risks (ATE) using double-robust estimator. difriskG Difference risks (ATE) using G-formula estimator. riskDR.iid, riskG.iid Influence functions marginal risk estimates. var, var.riskDR, var.riskG Variance-covariance matrices. se.coef, se.riskDR, se.riskG Standard errors.

title: Average Treatment Effect for Censored Competing Risks Data using Binomial Regression — binregATE
text: standard causal assumptions, ATE can estimated. assumptions include: Consistency: observed outcome equals potential outcome observed treatment. Ignorability: \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp | X\) (treatment assignment independent potential outcomes given covariates). Positivity: treatment levels non-zero probability given covariates. first covariate competing risks regression model must treatment variable, coded factor. factor two levels, multinomial logistic regression (mlogit) used propensity score modeling. absence censoring, reduces ordinary logistic regression. ATE estimated using standard doubly robust estimating equations IPCW-censoring adjusted. alternative binomial regression, logitIPCWATE provides IPCW-weighted version standard logistic regression. typeATE = "II", estimating equation augmented : $$ (/\pi(X)) \int E( O(t) | T \geq t, S(X))/ G_c(t,S(X)) d \hat M_c(s) $$ estimating mean outcome treated group.

code: data(prt) prt <- force_same_cens(prt,cause="status")  dd <- bicompriskData(Event(time, status)~strata(zyg)+id(id), data=prt, cause=c(2, 2)) newdata <- data.frame(zyg=c("DZ","MZ"),id=1)  ## concordance  bcif1 <- binreg(Event(time,status)~-1+factor(zyg)+cluster(id), data=dd,                 time=80, cause=1, cens.model=~strata(zyg)) pconc <- predict(bcif1,newdata)  ## marginal estimates  mbcif1 <- binreg(Event(time,status)~cluster(id), data=prt, time=80, cause=2) mc <- predict(mbcif1,newdata)  cse <- binregCasewise(bcif1,mbcif1) cse #> $coef #>     Estimate      2.5%     97.5% #> p1 0.1586277 0.1445496 0.1740770 #> p2 0.4041311 0.3682646 0.4434908 #>  #> $logcoef #>    Estimate Std.Err   2.5%   97.5%   P-value #> p1   -1.841 0.04742 -1.934 -1.7483 0.000e+00 #> p2   -0.906 0.04742 -0.999 -0.8131 2.208e-81 #>

code: binregRatio(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   time = NULL,   beta = NULL,   type = c("III", "II", "I"),   offset = NULL,   weights = NULL,   cens.weights = NULL,   cens.model = ~+1,   se = TRUE,   relative.to.causes = NULL,   kaplan.meier = TRUE,   cens.code = 0,   no.opt = FALSE,   method = "nr",   augmentation = NULL,   outcome = c("rmtl", "cif"),   model = c("logit", "exp", "lin"),   Ydirect = NULL,   ... )

title: Percentage of Years Lost Due to a Cause Regression — binregRatio
text: formula Formula outcome (see coxph). first covariate RHS typically treatment group indicator. Can include cluster(id). data Data frame containing variables. cause Numeric code cause interest. time Time point \(t\) analysis. Required. beta Starting values optimization (default NULL, uses zeros). type Type estimator: "" (IPCW ), "II" (IPCW + augmentation), "III" (IPCW + complex augmentation). Default "III". offset Offsets partial likelihood. weights Weights score equations. cens.weights External censoring weights (provided, cens.model ignored). cens.model Formula censoring model (default ~+1, stratified KM). Can include strata() stratified censoring. se Logical; TRUE, computes standard errors based IPCW (default TRUE). relative..causes NULL, compares RMTL specified cause RMTL causes vector (denominator becomes sum causes). kaplan.meier Logical; TRUE, uses Kaplan-Meier IPCW weights; FALSE, uses \(\exp(-\text{cumulative hazard})\). cens.code Censoring code (default 0). .opt Logical; TRUE, skips optimization uses beta directly. method Optimization method: "nr" (Newton-Raphson) "nlm". augmentation Initial augmentation term (used type "II" "III"). outcome Outcome type: "rmtl" (years lost) "cif" (cumulative incidence). model Link function: "logit" (default), "exp", "lin". Ydirect Matrix two columns (numerator, denominator) use directly response. ... Additional arguments passed lower-level functions.

title: Percentage of Years Lost Due to a Cause Regression — binregRatio
text: object class "binreg" "ratio" containing: coef Coefficient estimates. se.coef Standard errors. var Variance-covariance matrix. iid Influence function decomposition (censoring adjustment). iidI Influence function without censoring adjustment. naive.var Variance assuming known censoring. time Time point used. cause Cause interest. Causes Set causes considered denominator. Yipcw IPCW-adjusted response matrix. coefI, varI Results initial (type "") fit. augmentation Final augmentation term used.

title: Percentage of Years Lost Due to a Cause Regression — binregRatio
text: Let total years lost \(Y = t - \min(T, t)\) years lost due cause 1 \(Y_1 = (\epsilon=1) (t - \min(T, t))\). function models ratio: $$ \text{logit}\left( \frac{E(Y_1 | X)}{E(Y | X)} \right) = X^T \beta $$ Estimation based binomial regression IPCW response estimating equation: $$ X \left( \Delta^{\text{ipcw}}(t) \left( Y \cdot \text{expit}(X^T \beta) - Y_1 \right) \right) = 0 $$ \(\Delta^{\text{ipcw}}(t) = (\min(t,T) < C) / G_c(\min(t,T))\) IPCW adjustment. function supports three types estimators: "": Classical outcome IPCW regression (augmentation). "II": Adds censoring augmentation term \(X \int E(Z(t)| T>s)/G_c(s) d \hat M_c\)     improve efficiency (requires initial estimate \(\beta\)). "III": Adds complex augmentation term separating expectations     \(Y\) \(Y_1\) efficiency gains. variance based squared influence functions (IID). "naive" variance (assuming known censoring) also provided comparison.

title: Percentage of Years Lost Due to a Cause Regression — binregRatio

Percentage of total RMTL due to cause 1 rmtlratioI <- rmtlRatio(Event(time,cause)~platelet+tcell+age, bmt, time=30, cause=1) summary(rmtlratioI) #>    n events #>  408    154 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coeffients: #>              Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)  0.973135  0.177855  0.624545  1.321725  0.0000 #> platelet    -0.402875  0.323413 -1.036753  0.231002  0.2129 #> tcell       -0.845357  0.419214 -1.667002 -0.023712  0.0437 #> age          0.214462  0.168387 -0.115571  0.544495  0.2028 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> (Intercept)  2.64623 1.86740 3.7499 #> platelet     0.66840 0.35460 1.2599 #> tcell        0.42940 0.18881 0.9766 #> age          1.23920 0.89086 1.7237 #>  #>   newdata <- data.frame(platelet=1, tcell=1, age=1) pp <- predict(rmtlratioI, newdata) pp #>        pred        se    lower     upper #> 1 0.4848458 0.1092229 0.270769 0.6989226  ## Percentage of total cumulative incidence due to cause 1 cifratio <- binregRatio(Event(time,cause)~platelet+tcell+age, bmt, time=30, cause=1, model="cif") summary(cifratio) #>    n events #>  408    154 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coeffients: #>               Estimate    Std.Err       2.5%      97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)  0.7239521  0.0355963  0.6541846  0.7937196  0.0000 #> platelet    -0.0889619  0.0730427 -0.2321228  0.0541991  0.2232 #> tcell       -0.1951701  0.1005726 -0.3922888  0.0019486  0.0523 #> age          0.0452930  0.0358278 -0.0249281  0.1155142  0.2062 #>  #>  #>  pp <- predict(cifratio, newdata) pp #>        pred        se     lower     upper #> 1 0.4851132 0.1050915 0.2791377 0.6910887

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
text: Estimates average treatment effect \(E(Y(,j))\) treatment regime \((,j)\) two-stage randomization. estimator can augmented using information randomizations dynamic censoring augmentation improve efficiency.

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
code: binregTSR(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   time = NULL,   cens.code = 0,   response.code = NULL,   augmentR0 = NULL,   treat.model0 = ~+1,   augmentR1 = NULL,   treat.model1 = ~+1,   augmentC = NULL,   cens.model = ~+1,   estpr = c(1, 1),   response.name = NULL,   offset = NULL,   weights = NULL,   cens.weights = NULL,   beta = NULL,   kaplan.meier = TRUE,   no.opt = FALSE,   method = "nr",   augmentation = NULL,   outcome = c("cif", "rmst", "rmst-cause"),   model = "exp",   Ydirect = NULL,   return.dataw = 0,   pi0 = 0.5,   pi1 = 0.5,   cens.time.fixed = 1,   outcome.iid = 1,   meanCs = 0,   ... )

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
text: formula Formula outcome (see coxph), typically Event(entry,time,status)~+1+cluster(id). data Data frame containing variables. cause Cause interest competing risks (default 1). time Time point estimation. cens.code Censoring code (default 0). response.code Code status indicating response 2nd randomization occurs. augmentR0 Covariates augmentation model first randomization. treat.model0 Logistic treatment model first randomization. augmentR1 Covariates augmentation model second randomization. treat.model1 Logistic treatment model second randomization. augmentC Covariates censoring augmentation model. cens.model Stratification censoring model based observed covariates. estpr Logical; estimate randomization probabilities using model (default TRUE). response.name Name response variable (reads treat.model1 NULL). offset implemented. weights implemented. cens.weights Can provided externally. beta Starting values optimization. kaplan.meier Logical; use Kaplan-Meier censoring weights rather exp cumulative hazard. .opt implemented. method implemented. augmentation implemented. outcome Outcome type: "cif" (cumulative incidence), "rmst" (restricted mean survival time), "rmst-cause" (restricted mean time lost cause). model implemented, uses linear regression augmentation. Ydirect Use Y instead outcome constructed inside program. return.dataw Logical; return weighted data treatment regimes. pi0 Known randomization probabilities first randomization. pi1 Known randomization probabilities second randomization. cens.time.fixed Logical; use time-dependent weights censoring estimation. outcome.iid Logical; get iid contribution outcome model. meanCs Logical; indicates censoring augmentation centered CensAugment.times/n. ... Additional arguments lower-level functions.

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
text: object class "binregTSR" containing: riskG Simple estimator results (coefficient, SE). riskG0 First randomization augmentation results. riskG1 Second randomization augmentation results. riskG01 randomizations augmentation results. riskG.iid Influence functions estimators. varG Variance-covariance matrices. MGc Censoring martingale contributions. CensAugment.times Censoring augmentation terms. dynCens.coef Dynamic censoring coefficients. dataW Weighted data (return.dataw=TRUE).

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
text: method solves estimating equation: $$ \frac{(\min(T_i,t) < G_i)}{G_c(\min(T_i,t))} (T \leq t, \epsilon=1) - AUG_0 - AUG_1 + AUG_C - p(,j) = 0 $$ : \(AUG_0 = \frac{A_0() - \pi_0()}{\pi_0()} X_0 \gamma_0\) uses covariates augmentR0 \(AUG_1 = \frac{A_0()}{\pi_0()} \frac{A_1(j) - \pi_1(j)}{\pi_1(j)} X_1 \gamma_1\) uses covariates augmentR1 \(AUG_C = \int_0^t \gamma_c(s)^T (e(s) - \bar e(s)) \frac{1}{G_c(s)} dM_c(s)\) censoring augmentation Standard errors estimated using influence function estimators, enabling tests differences computed subsequently. method handles survival data competing risks data, supports multiple treatment levels.

title: Two-Stage Randomization for Survival or Competing Risks Data — binregTSR
text: Scheike, T. H. (2024). Two-stage randomization analysis survival data. mets package documentation.

code: ddf <- mets:::gsim(200,covs=1,null=0,cens=1,ce=2)  bb <- binregTSR(Event(entry,time,status)~+1+cluster(id),ddf$datat,time=2,cause=c(1),         cens.code=0,treat.model0=A0.f~+1,treat.model1=A1.f~A0.f,         augmentR1=~X11+X12+TR,augmentR0=~X01+X02,         augmentC=~A01+A02+X01+X02+A11t+A12t+X11+X12+TR,         response.code=2) summary(bb)  #> Simple estimator : #>                              coef            #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=1 0.7965990 0.06876063 #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=2 0.7290403 0.05904496 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=1 0.2652459 0.08526775 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=2 0.3463623 0.07507682 #>  #> First Randomization Augmentation : #>                              coef            #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=1 0.8020553 0.07037422 #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=2 0.7380517 0.05897235 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=1 0.2643500 0.08356227 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=2 0.3392007 0.07533492 #>  #> Second Randomization Augmentation : #>                              coef            #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=1 0.8270658 0.05679816 #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=2 0.7414130 0.06070947 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=1 0.2497165 0.09169221 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=2 0.3645886 0.07180896 #>  #> 1st and 2nd Randomization Augmentation : #>                              coef            #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=1 0.8352750 0.05697325 #> A0.f=1, response*A1.f=2 0.7531912 0.05912032 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=1 0.2495341 0.08954463 #> A0.f=2, response*A1.f=2 0.3630083 0.07086160 #>

title: Bivariate Probit model — biprobit
code: data(prt) prt0 <- subset(prt,country=="Denmark") a <- biprobit(cancer~1+zyg, ~1+zyg, data=prt0, id="id") predict(a, newdata=lava::Expand(prt, zyg=c("MZ"))) #>           p11        p10        p01       p00         p1         p2       mu1 #> 1 0.005649701 0.01034772 0.01034772 0.9736549 0.01599742 0.01599742 -2.144475 #>         mu2       rho parameter zyg #> 1 -2.144475 0.7547259         1  MZ ## b <- biprobit(cancer~1+zyg, ~1+zyg, data=prt0, id="id",pairs.only=TRUE) ## predict(b,newdata=lava::Expand(prt,zyg=c("MZ","DZ")))   ## Reduce Ex.Timings n <- 2e3 x <- sort(runif(n, -1, 1)) y <- rmvn(n, c(0,0), rho=cbind(tanh(x)))>0 d <- data.frame(y1=y[,1], y2=y[,2], x=x) dd <- fast.reshape(d)  a <- biprobit(y~1+x,rho=~1+x,data=dd,id="id") summary(a, mean.contrast=c(1,.5), cor.contrast=c(1,.5)) #>  #>                 Estimate    Std.Err       Z p-value     #> (Intercept)    0.0035356  0.0198806  0.1778 0.85885     #> x             -0.0755638  0.0333240 -2.2676 0.02336 *   #> r:(Intercept)  0.0091359  0.0382962  0.2386 0.81145     #> r:x            1.0296248  0.0726052 14.1811 < 2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> logLik: -2650.936  mean(score^2): 3.114e-05  #>     n pairs  #>  4000  2000  #>  #> Contrast: #> 	Dependence    [(Intercept)] + 0.5[x]  #> 	Mean          [(Intercept)] + 0.5[x]  #>  #>                         Estimate 2.5%    97.5%   #> Rel.Recur.Risk          1.33713  1.26918 1.40508 #> OR                      3.75680  2.91262 4.84565 #> Tetrachoric correlation 0.48074  0.39827 0.55550 #>                                                  #> Concordance             0.31627  0.28970 0.34409 #> Casewise Concordance    0.65030  0.61607 0.68305 #> Marginal                0.48634  0.46289 0.50985 with(predict(a,data.frame(x=seq(-1,1,by=.1))), plot(p00~x,type="l"))   pp <- predict(a,data.frame(x=seq(-1,1,by=.1)),which=c(1)) plot(pp[,1]~pp$x, type="l", xlab="x", ylab="Concordance", lwd=2, xaxs="i") lava::confband(pp$x,pp[,2],pp[,3],polygon=TRUE,lty=0,col=lava::Col(1))   pp <- predict(a,data.frame(x=seq(-1,1,by=.1)),which=c(9)) ## rho plot(pp[,1]~pp$x, type="l", xlab="x", ylab="Correlation", lwd=2, xaxs="i") lava::confband(pp$x,pp[,2],pp[,3],polygon=TRUE,lty=0,col=lava::Col(1)) with(pp, lines(x,tanh(-x),lwd=2,lty=2))  xp <- seq(-1,1,length.out=6); delta <- mean(diff(xp)) a2 <- biprobit(y~1+x,rho=~1+I(cut(x,breaks=xp)),data=dd,id="id") pp2 <- predict(a2,data.frame(x=xp[-1]-delta/2),which=c(9)) ## rho lava::confband(pp2$x,pp2[,2],pp2[,3],center=pp2[,1])      ## Time if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{     a <- biprobit.time(cancer~1, rho=~1+zyg, id="id", data=prt, eqmarg=TRUE,                        cens.formula=Surv(time,status==0)~1,                        breaks=seq(75,100,by=3),fix.censweights=TRUE)      a <- biprobit.time2(cancer~1+zyg, rho=~1+zyg, id="id", data=prt0, eqmarg=TRUE,                        cens.formula=Surv(time,status==0)~zyg,                        breaks=100)      #a1 <- biprobit.time2(cancer~1, rho=~1, id="id", data=subset(prt0,zyg=="MZ"), eqmarg=TRUE,     #                   cens.formula=Surv(time,status==0)~1,     #                   breaks=100,pairs.only=TRUE)      #a2 <- biprobit.time2(cancer~1, rho=~1, id="id", data=subset(prt0,zyg=="DZ"), eqmarg=TRUE,     #                    cens.formula=Surv(time,status==0)~1,     #                    breaks=100,pairs.only=TRUE) } # }

title: Non-parametric Cumulative Incidence Functions — rr_cif
text: cif cumulative incidence model object (timereg). data data.frame variables. cause causes plot. cif2 optional second CIF model different first. times time points evaluation. cause1 cause first coordinate. cause2 cause second coordinate. cens.code censoring code value. cens.model censoring model type (default "KM"). Nit maximum number iterations. detail level output detail. clusters cluster variable name vector. theta dependence parameter(s). theta.des design matrix theta. step step size optimization. sym 1, symmetric dependence structure. weights optional weights. .cens logical; TRUE, uses joint censoring weights. censoring.weights optional pre-computed censoring weights. silent verbosity level. par.func optional parameter function. dpar.func optional derivative parameter function. dimpar dimension parameter vector. score.method optimization method (default "nlminb"). entry optional entry time variable. estimator estimator type. trunkp truncation probability. admin.cens administrative censoring time. ... additional arguments. cif1 CIF values subject 1 (predictPairPlack). status1 status subject 1. status2 status subject 2. T matrix columns: time1, time2, status1, status2 (npc). sep logical; TRUE, uses separate censoring models subject. t vector event/censoring times (nonparcuminc). status vector status codes (nonparcuminc). cens censoring code (default 0). x data matrix competing risks object. col colors curves. lty line types curves. legend logical; TRUE, add legend. plots show. ask logical; TRUE, prompt new page.

title: Non-parametric Cumulative Incidence Functions — rr_cif

Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
text: Fits regression model cumulative incidence function (CIF) presence competing risks. Supports two link functions: propodds=1 (default): Logistic link model (logit CIF), providing Odds Ratio () interpretations. propodds=NULL: Fine-Gray (cloglog) regression model, providing subdistribution hazard ratio interpretations.

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
code: cifreg(   formula,   data,   propodds = 1,   cause = 1,   cens.code = 0,   no.codes = NULL,   death.code = NULL,   ... )

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
text: formula Formula 'Event' outcome. data Data frame containing variables. propodds Logical; 1 (default), fits logit link model. NULL, fits Fine-Gray model. cause Cause interest (default 1). cens.code Code censoring (default 0). .codes Event codes ignored identifying competing causes (useful administrative censoring). death.code Codes death (terminal events). NULL, defaults remaining codes (excluding cause, cens.code, .codes). ... Additional arguments passed recreg.

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
text: object class "cifreg" (extending "phreg") containing: coef Estimated coefficients. var Robust variance-covariance matrix. iid Influence functions coefficients. cumhaz Cumulative incidence estimates. propodds Indicator link function used.

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
text: Fine-Gray model, score equations : $$ \int (X - E(t)) Y_1(t) w(t) dM_1 $$ summed clusters returned iid.naive (multiplied inverse second derivative). , $$w(t) = G(t) ((T_i \wedge t < C_i)/G_c(T_i \wedge t))$$, $$E(t) = S_1(t)/S_0(t)$$, $$S_j(t) = \sum X_i^j Y_{i1}(t) w_i(t) \exp(X_i^T \beta)$$. full influence function (IID decomposition) beta coefficients includes censoring term: $$ \int (X - E(t)) Y_1(t) w(t) dM_1 + \int q(s)/p(s) dM_c $$ returned iid component. logistic link model, standard errors may slightly underestimated uncertainty recursive baseline estimation fully accounted . smaller datasets, recommended use prop.odds.subdist function timereg package (uses efficient weights) bootstrap standard errors.

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Cumulative Incidence Function (CIF) Regression — cifreg
code: ## data with no ties data(bmt,package="mets") bmt$time <- bmt$time+runif(nrow(bmt))*0.01 bmt$id <- 1:nrow(bmt)  ## logistic link  OR interpretation or=cifreg(Event(time,cause)~tcell+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1) summary(or) #>  #>    n events #>  408    161 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coefficients: #>           Estimate      S.E.   dU^-1/2 P-value #> tcell    -0.709607  0.331978  0.274926  0.0326 #> platelet -0.455297  0.236017  0.187920  0.0537 #> age       0.391175  0.098036  0.083670  0.0001 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>          Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> tcell     0.49184 0.25659 0.9428 #> platelet  0.63426 0.39936 1.0073 #> age       1.47872 1.22022 1.7920 #>  par(mfrow=c(1,2)) plot(or) nd <- data.frame(tcell=c(1,0),platelet=0,age=0) por <- predict(or,nd) plot(por)   ## approximate standard errors  por <-mets:::predict.phreg(or,nd) plot(por,se=1)  ## Fine-Gray model fg=cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~tcell+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1) summary(fg) #>  #>    n events #>  408    161 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coefficients: #>           Estimate      S.E.   dU^-1/2 P-value #> tcell    -0.597055  0.270452  0.275783  0.0273 #> platelet -0.426037  0.180707  0.187720  0.0184 #> age       0.343921  0.080270  0.086285  0.0000 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>          Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> tcell     0.55043 0.32396 0.9352 #> platelet  0.65309 0.45831 0.9307 #> age       1.41047 1.20514 1.6508 #>  ##fg=recreg(Event(time,cause)~tcell+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1,death.code=2) ##summary(fg) plot(fg)  nd <- data.frame(tcell=c(1,0),platelet=0,age=0) pfg <- predict(fg,nd,se=1) plot(pfg,se=1)  ## bt <- iidBaseline(fg,time=30) ## bt <- IIDrecreg(fg$cox.prep,fg,time=30)  ## not run to avoid timing issues ## gofFG(Event(time,cause)~tcell+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1)  sfg <- cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1) summary(sfg) #>  #>    n events #>  408    161 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coefficients: #>           Estimate      S.E.   dU^-1/2 P-value #> platelet -0.424645  0.180792  0.187822  0.0188 #> age       0.342094  0.079862  0.086285  0.0000 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>          Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> platelet  0.65400 0.45887 0.9321 #> age       1.40789 1.20390 1.6465 #>  plot(sfg)   ### predictions with CI based on iid decomposition of baseline and beta ### these are used in the predict function above fg <- cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~tcell+platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1) Biid <- iidBaseline(fg,time=20) pfg1 <- FGprediid(Biid,nd) pfg1 #>           pred     se-log     lower     upper #> [1,] 0.2692822 0.22756923 0.1723853 0.4206442 #> [2,] 0.4344567 0.07477993 0.3752267 0.5030363

title: Concordance Computes concordance and casewise concordance — concordanceCor
text: concordance probability twins experienced event interest defined $$  cor(t) = P(T_1 \leq t, \epsilon_1 =1 , T_2 \leq t, \epsilon_2=1)  $$ Similarly, casewise concordance $$  casewise(t) = \frac{cor(t)}{P(T_1 \leq t, \epsilon_1=1) } $$ probability twin "2" event given twins "1" .

what: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
title: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
text: Fits parametric model log-cross-odds-ratio predictive effect cumulative incidence curves \(T_1\) experiencing cause given \(T_2\) experienced cause k : $$ \log(COR(|k))  =  h(\theta,z_1,,z_2,k,t)=_{default}  \theta^T z = $$ log cross odds ratio $$ COR(|k) = \frac{O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=| T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)}{ O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=)} $$ conditional odds divided unconditional odds, odds , respectively $$ O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=| T_2 \leq t,cause_1=k) = \frac{ P_x(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=| T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)}{ P_x((T_1 \leq t,cause_1=)^c | T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)} $$ $$ O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=) = \frac{P_x(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=)}{P_x((T_1 \leq t,cause_1=)^c )}. $$ \(B^c\) complement event \(B\), \(P_x\) distribution given covariates (\(x\) subject specific \(z\) cluster specific covariates), \(h()\) function simple identity \(\theta^T z\) default.

title: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
code: cor_cif(   cif,   data,   cause = NULL,   times = NULL,   cause1 = 1,   cause2 = 1,   cens.code = NULL,   cens.model = "KM",   Nit = 40,   detail = 0,   clusters = NULL,   theta = NULL,   theta.des = NULL,   step = 1,   sym = 0,   weights = NULL,   par.func = NULL,   dpar.func = NULL,   dimpar = NULL,   score.method = "nlminb",   same.cens = FALSE,   censoring.weights = NULL,   silent = 1,   ... )

title: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
text: cif model object timereg::comp.risk function marginal cumulative incidence cause1, .e., event interest, whose odds comparision compared conditional odds given cause2 data data.frame variables. cause specifies causes  related death times, value cens.code censoring value. missing comes marginal cif. times time-vector specifies times used estimating euqations cross-odds-ratio estimation. cause1 specificies cause considered. cause2 specificies cause conditioned . cens.code specificies code censoring NULL uses one marginal cif model. cens.model specified model use ICPW, KM Kaplan-Meier alternatively may "cox" Nit number iterations Newton-Raphson algorithm. detail 0 details printed iterations, 1 details given. clusters specifies cluster structure. theta specifies starting values cross-odds-ratio parameters model. theta.des specifies regression design cross-odds-ratio parameters. step specifies step size Newton-Raphson algorithm. sym specifies symmetry used model. weights weights estimating equations. par.func parfunc dpar.func dparfunc dimpar dimpar score.method "nlminb", can also use "nr". .cens true censoring within clusters assumed variable, default independent censoring. censoring.weights probabilities used bivariate censoring dist. silent 1 suppress output convergence related issues. ... used.

title: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
text: returns object type 'cor'. following arguments: theta estimate proportional odds parameters model. var.theta variance gamma. hess derivative used score. score scores final stage. score scores final stage. theta.iid matrix iid decomposition parametric effects.

title: Cross-odds-ratio, OR or RR risk regression for competing risks — cor_cif
text: dependence measure given $$ (,k) = \log ( \frac{O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=| T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)}{ O(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=) | T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)} $$ measure numerically stabile COR measure, symetric ,k. RR dependence measure given $$ RR(,k) = \log ( \frac{P(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=, T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)}{ P(T_1 \leq t,cause_1=) P(T_2 \leq t,cause_2=k)} $$ measure numerically stabile COR measure, symetric ,k. model fitted symmetry (sym=1), .e., assumed \(T_1\) \(T_2\) can interchanged leads cross-odd-ratio (.e. \(COR(|k) = COR(k|))\), expected twins without symmetry might case mothers daughters (sym=0). \(h()\) may specified R-function parameters, see example , default simply \(\theta^T z\).

title: Compute cumulative event counts as time-dependent covariates — count_history
text: data data frame counting-process format, one row per event interval per subject. status Name column containing event status codes. Default "status". id Name column containing subject identifiers. Default "id". types Integer vector status codes count. value types generates one new count column (multitype = FALSE) contributes single combined count (multitype = TRUE). Default 1. names.count Prefix names new count columns. status code appended, e.g. "Count1", "Count2". Default "Count". lag Logical. TRUE (default), count row number events strictly current time (\(N(t-)\)). FALSE, events current time included (\(N(t)\)). multitype Logical. TRUE, events status types aggregated single count column, optionally weighted marks. FALSE (default), separate count column created value types. marks Optional numeric vector weights applied events multitype = TRUE. NULL (default), event weight 1.

title: Compute cumulative event counts as time-dependent covariates — count_history
text: input data frame data one new integer columns   appended. multitype = FALSE, columns named   paste0(names.count, k) k types;   multitype = TRUE, single column named   paste0(names.count, types[1]) added. internal bookkeeping   column lbnr__id also added.

title: Compute cumulative event counts as time-dependent covariates — count_history

title: The Diabetic Retinopathy Data — diabetes
text: data colleceted test laser treatment delaying blindness patients dibetic retinopathy. subset 197 patiens given Huster et al. (1989) used.

title: The Diabetic Retinopathy Data — diabetes
text: data frame contains following columns: id numeric vector. Patient code. agedx numeric vector. Age patient diagnosis. time numeric vector. Survival time: time blindness censoring. status numeric vector code. Survival status. 1: blindness, 0: censored. trteye numeric vector code. Random eye selected treatment. 1: left eye 2: right eye. treat numeric vector. 1: treatment 0: untreated. adult numeric vector code. 1: younger 20, 2: older 20.

title: The Diabetic Retinopathy Data — diabetes
text: Huster W.J. Brookmeyer, R. Self. S. (1989) MOdelling paired survival data covariates, Biometrics 45, 145-56.

title: The Diabetic Retinopathy Data — diabetes
code: data(diabetes) names(diabetes) #> [1] "id"     "time"   "status" "trteye" "treat"  "adult"  "agedx"

Additional arguments fun

title: Regression for data frames with dutility call — dreg
text: Klaus K. Holst, Thomas Scheike

title: Regression for data frames with dutility call — dreg
code: ##' data(iris) dat <- iris drename(dat) <- ~. names(dat) #> [1] "sepal.length" "sepal.width"  "petal.length" "petal.width"  "species"      set.seed(1) dat$time <- runif(nrow(dat)) dat$time1 <- runif(nrow(dat)) dat$status <- rbinom(nrow(dat),1,0.5) dat$S1 <- with(dat, Surv(time,status)) dat$S2 <- with(dat, Surv(time1,status)) dat$id <- 1:nrow(dat)  mm <- dreg(dat, "*.length"~"*.width"|I(species=="setosa" & status==1)) mm <- dreg(dat, "*.length"~"*.width"|species+status) mm <- dreg(dat, "*.length"~"*.width"|species) mm <- dreg(dat, "*.length"~"*.width"|species+status,z.arg="group")   ## Reduce Ex.Timings y <- "S*"~"*.width" xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg) ## xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=survdiff)  y <- "S*"~"*.width" xs <- dreg(dat, y, x.oneatatime=FALSE, fun.=phreg)  ## under condition y <- S1~"*.width"|I(species=="setosa" & sepal.width>3) xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="condition", fun.=phreg) xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ## under condition y <- S1~"*.width"|species=="setosa" xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="condition", fun.=phreg) xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ## with baseline  after | y <- S1~"*.width"|sepal.length xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ## by group by species, not working y <- S1~"*.width"|species ss <- split(dat, paste(dat$species, dat$status))  xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ## species as base, species is factor so assumes that this is grouping y <- S1~"*.width"|species xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="base", fun.=phreg)  ##  background var after | and then one of x's at at time y <- S1~"*.width"|status+"sepal*" xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ##  background var after | and then one of x's at at time ##y <- S1~"*.width"|status+"sepal*" ##xs <- dreg(dat, y, x.oneatatime=FALSE, fun.=phreg) ##xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg)  ##  background var after | and then one of x's at at time ##y <- S1~"*.width"+factor(species) ##xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg) ##xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=phreg, x.oneatatime=FALSE)  y <- S1~"*.width"|factor(species) xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="base", fun.=phreg)  y <- S1~"*.width"|cluster(id)+factor(species) xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="base", fun.=phreg) xs <- dreg(dat, y, z.arg="base", fun.=survival::coxph)  ## under condition with groups y <- S1~"*.width"|I(sepal.length>4) xs <- dreg(subset(dat, species=="setosa"), y,z.arg="group",fun.=phreg)  ## under condition with groups y <- S1~"*.width"+I(log(sepal.length))|I(sepal.length>4) xs <- dreg(subset(dat, species=="setosa"), y,z.arg="group",fun.=phreg)  y <- S1~"*.width"+I(dcut(sepal.length))|I(sepal.length>4) xs <- dreg(subset(dat,species=="setosa"), y,z.arg="group",fun.=phreg)  ff <- function(formula,data,...) {  ss <- survfit(formula,data,...)  kmplot(ss,...)  return(ss) }  if (interactive()) { dcut(dat) <- ~"*.width" y <- S1~"*.4"|I(sepal.length>4) par(mfrow=c(1, 2)) xs <- dreg(dat, y, fun.=ff) }

title: Simple linear spline — dspline
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simple linear spline — dspline
code: data(TRACE) TRACE <- dspline(TRACE,~wmi,breaks=c(1,1.3,1.7)) cca <- survival::coxph(Surv(time,status==9)~age+vf+chf+wmi,data=TRACE) cca2 <- survival::coxph(Surv(time,status==9)~age+wmi+vf+chf+                            wmi.spline1+wmi.spline2+wmi.spline3,data=TRACE) anova(cca,cca2) #> Analysis of Deviance Table #>  Cox model: response is  Surv(time, status == 9) #>  Model 1: ~ age + vf + chf + wmi #>  Model 2: ~ age + wmi + vf + chf + wmi.spline1 + wmi.spline2 + wmi.spline3 #>    loglik  Chisq Df Pr(>|Chi|)     #> 1 -6529.8                          #> 2 -6519.6 20.323  3  0.0001455 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1  nd <- data.frame(age=50,vf=0,chf=0,wmi=seq(0.4,3,by=0.01)) nd <- dspline(nd,~wmi,breaks=c(1,1.3,1.7)) pl <- predict(cca2,newdata=nd) plot(nd$wmi,pl,type="l")

what: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: Performs cumulative score process residual tests proportional hazards (PH) assumption Cox regression. test statistics based cumulative score process: $$ U(t) = \int_0^t (X_i - E(t) ) d \hat M_i(s) $$ \(\hat M_i(s)\) martingale residuals.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
code: # S3 method for class 'phreg' gof(object, n.sim = 1000, silent = 1, robust = NULL, ...)

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: object fitted phreg object (mets survival). n.sim Number simulations resampling procedure (default 1000). silent Logical; TRUE, suppresses timing estimates long jobs. robust Logical; TRUE, uses robust martingale-based simulations. NULL, defaults TRUE cluster term detected model call. ... Additional arguments passed lower-level functions.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: object class "gof.phreg" containing: jumptimes Event times used process. supUsim Matrix simulated supremum values covariate. res Matrix observed supremum (Sup|U(t)|) p-values. supU Observed supremum values. pvals Vector p-values covariate. score Cumulative score process values time. simUt Simulated score processes. type Type test performed ("prop"). robust Logical flag indicating robust simulation used.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: P-values computed using Lin, Wei, Ying (1993) resampling method, simulates asymptotic distribution supremum score process null hypothesis proportional hazards. function supports two types simulation: Standard: Uses \(dN_i\) (counting process increments) simulation. Robust: Uses \(\hat M_i(t)\) (martingale residuals) adjusted clustering     cluster() term present model.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: Lin, D. Y., Wei, L. J., & Ying, Z. (1993). Checking Cox model cumulative sums martingale-based residuals. Biometrika, 80(3), 557-572.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
text: Thomas Scheike Klaus K. Holst

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox PH Regression (Proportionality) — gof.phreg
code: data(sTRACE)  m1 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~vf+chf+diabetes, data=sTRACE)  gg <- gof(m1) gg #> Cumulative score process test for Proportionality: #>          Sup|U(t)|  pval #> vf        7.276731 0.009 #> chf       8.971263 0.078 #> diabetes  3.044404 0.788 par(mfrow=c(1,3)) plot(gg)   m1 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~strata(vf)+chf+diabetes, data=sTRACE)  gg <- gof(m1)  ## Robust simulations with cluster sTRACE$id <- 1:500 m1 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~vf+chf+diabetes+cluster(id), data=sTRACE)  gg <- gof(m1) gg #> Cumulative score process test for Proportionality: #>          Sup|U(t)|  pval #> vf        7.276731 0.004 #> chf       8.971263 0.067 #> diabetes  3.044404 0.790

Typically constructed using cumContr() manually (e.g., quartiles continuous covariate). n.sim Number simulations (default 1000). silent Logical; suppresses timing estimates TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed phreg.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox Covariates (Model Matrix) — gofM_phreg
text: object class "gof.phreg" containing: jumptimes Event times. supUsim Simulated supremum values. res Matrix observed supremum p-values column modelmatrix. score Cumulative score process. simUt Simulated processes. Utlast, pval.last Supremum p-value final time point (covariate direction). type Type test ("modelmatrix").

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox Covariates (Model Matrix) — gofM_phreg
text: test statistic : $$ U(t) = \int_0^t K^T d \hat M $$ \(K\) model matrix (e.g., set basis functions continuous covariate) \(\hat M\) martingale residuals. P-values based Lin, Wei, Ying (1993) resampling method. plot shows whether residuals consistent model across range covariate.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox Covariates (Model Matrix) — gofM_phreg
text: Lin, D. Y., Wei, L. J., & Ying, Z. (1993). Checking Cox model cumulative sums martingale-based residuals. Biometrika, 80(3), 557-572.

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox Covariates (Model Matrix) — gofM_phreg
text: Thomas Scheike Klaus K. Holst

title: Goodness-of-Fit for Cox Covariates (Model Matrix) — gofM_phreg
code: data(TRACE) set.seed(1) TRACEsam <- blocksample(TRACE, idvar="id", replace=FALSE, 100) dcut(TRACEsam) <- ~.  mm <- model.matrix(~-1+factor(wmicat.4), data=TRACEsam) m1 <- gofM_phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~vf+chf+wmi, data=TRACEsam, modelmatrix=mm) summary(m1) #> Cumulative residuals versus modelmatrix : #>                            Sup_t |U(t)|  pval #> factor(wmicat.4)[0.4,1.1]      5.788752 0.021 #> factor(wmicat.4)(1.1,1.4]      2.633143 0.591 #> factor(wmicat.4)(1.4,1.72]     5.657370 0.038 #> factor(wmicat.4)(1.72,2]       1.227485 0.948 #>  #> Cumulative score process versus covariates (discrete z via model.matrix): #>         Sup_z |U(tau,z)|  pval #> matrixZ         1.801626 0.812 if (interactive()) { par(mfrow=c(2,2)) plot(m1) }  ## Cumulative sums in covariates via design matrix mm <- mets:::cumContr(TRACEsam$wmi, breaks=10, equi=TRUE) m1 <- gofM_phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~strata(vf)+chf+wmi, data=TRACEsam,          modelmatrix=mm, silent=0) #> Cumulative score process test for modelmatrix: #>        Sup_t |U(t)| pval #> <=0.56         0.88 0.33 #> <=0.72         2.25 0.26 #> <=0.88         5.28 0.03 #> <=1.04         2.68 0.54 #> <=1.2          4.12 0.17 #> <=1.36         4.09 0.17 #> <=1.52         3.34 0.24 #> <=1.68         1.78 0.83 #> <=1.84         1.17 0.84 #> <=2            0.00 1.00 summary(m1) #> Cumulative residuals versus modelmatrix : #>        Sup_t |U(t)|  pval #> <=0.56    0.8821214 0.327 #> <=0.72    2.2521756 0.263 #> <=0.88    5.2766972 0.031 #> <=1.04    2.6807567 0.542 #> <=1.2     4.1217296 0.174 #> <=1.36    4.0926272 0.173 #> <=1.52    3.3447151 0.242 #> <=1.68    1.7766131 0.831 #> <=1.84    1.1652990 0.839 #> <=2       0.0000000 1.000 #>  #> Cumulative score process versus covariates (discrete z via model.matrix): #>         Sup_z |U(tau,z)| pval #> matrixZ         3.931466 0.23

text: test statistic : $$ U(z, \tau) = \int_0^\tau K(z)^T d \hat M $$ \(K(z)\) design matrix based indicator functions \((Z_i \leq z_l)\) grid points \(z_l\). p-value valid depends chosen grid. number break points increases, test converges original Lin, Wei, Ying test linearity.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: Performs cycle-specific logistic regression estimate haplotype effects discrete time--event data, accounting phase ambiguity. Given observed genotypes \(G\) unobserved haplotypes \(H\), method integrates (mixes ) possible haplotype configurations using conditional probabilities \(P(H|G)\).

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
code: haplo_surv_discrete(   X = NULL,   y = "y",   time.name = "time",   Haplos = NULL,   id = "id",   desnames = NULL,   designfunc = NULL,   beta = NULL,   no.opt = FALSE,   method = "NR",   stderr = TRUE,   designMatrix = NULL,   response = NULL,   idhap = NULL,   design.only = FALSE,   covnames = NULL,   fam = binomial,   weights = NULL,   offsets = NULL,   idhapweights = NULL,   ... )

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: X Design matrix data frame (must sorted id time) containing ID, time variable, binary response, covariates. y Name response variable (binary, 0/1) X. time.name Name time variable X used sorting cycle definition. Haplos Data frame containing id, haplo1, haplo2 (haplotypes factors), p (probability \(P(H|G)\)). id Name ID variable X Haplos. desnames Names covariate columns X used design matrix. designfunc Function computes design vector given haplotypes \(h=(h_1, h_2)\) covariates. Must return vector matrix compatible model. beta Starting values optimization (vector length \(p + k\), \(p\) number covariate effects \(k\) number time cycles). .opt Logical; TRUE, skips optimization returns estimates based provided beta (useful initialization diagnostics). method Optimization method: "NR" (Newton-Raphson, default) "nlm". stderr Logical; FALSE, returns coefficient estimates. designMatrix Alternative X Haplos: provides response design matrix directly (fully implemented). response Alternative X: provides response design directly (fully implemented). idhap Name ID-haplotype variable specify different haplotypes different IDs. design.Logical; TRUE, returns design matrices constructed analysis. covnames Names covariates extract object regression output. fam Family model (default binomial, currently option). weights Weights following ID GLM component. offsets Offsets following ID GLM component. idhapweights Weights following ID-haplotype GLM component (Work Progress). ... Additional arguments passed optimizer (lava::NR nlm).

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: object class "haplosurvd" containing: coef Estimated coefficients (baseline time effects haplotype/covariate effects). se Standard errors coefficients. var Variance-covariance matrix. se.robust Robust standard errors (available). iid Influence function (IID) decomposition. ploglik Log-likelihood convergence. gradient, hessian Optimization results. Xhap, X, Haplos Data design matrices used. nid, nidhap Number IDs ID-haplotype combinations.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: survival function computed averaging possible haplotypes: $$ S(t|x,G) = E[ S(t|x,H) | G ] = \sum_{h \G} P(h|G) S(t|x,h) $$ discrete hazard function modeled using logistic regression: $$ \text{logit}(P(T=t | T \geq t, x, h)) = \alpha_t + x(h) \beta $$ \(\alpha_t\) time-specific intercepts (baseline hazards), \(x(h)\) regression design constructed covariates haplotypes \(h=(h_1, h_2)\), \(\beta\) regression coefficients. likelihood maximized numerically. Standard errors computed assuming \(P(H|G)\) known (.e., ignoring uncertainty haplotype estimation). design matrix possible haplotypes constructed merging covariate data \(X\) haplotype probabilities \(Haplos\) applying user-defined designfunc.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: Scheike, T. H. (2024). Discrete time survival analysis haplotype effects. mets package documentation.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Haplotype Analysis — haplo_surv_discrete
code: ## Some haplotypes of interest types <- c("DCGCGCTCACG","DTCCGCTGACG","ITCAGTTGACG","ITCCGCTGAGG")  ## Some haplotype frequencies for simulations  data(haplo) hapfreqs <- haplo$hapfreqs   www <- which(hapfreqs$haplotype %in% types) hapfreqs$freq[www] #> [1] 0.138387 0.103394 0.048124 0.291273  baseline <- hapfreqs$haplotype[9] baseline #> [1] "DTGCGCTCGCG"  ## Design function: indicator for presence of any 'types' haplotype designftypes <- function(x, sm=0) {   hap1 <- x[1]   hap2 <- x[2]   if (sm == 0) y <- 1 * ((hap1 == types) | (hap2 == types))   if (sm == 1) y <- 1 * (hap1 == types) + 1 * (hap2 == types)   return(y) }  tcoef <- c(-1.93110204, -0.47531630, -0.04118204, -1.57872602, -0.22176426, -0.13836416,            0.88830288, 0.60756224, 0.39802821, 0.32706859)  ghaplos <- haplo$ghaplos haploX  <- haplo$haploX  haploX$time <- haploX$times Xdes <- model.matrix(~ factor(time), haploX) colnames(Xdes) <- paste("X", 1:ncol(Xdes), sep="") X <- dkeep(haploX, ~ id + y + time) X <- cbind(X, Xdes) Haplos <- dkeep(ghaplos, ~ id + "haplo*" + p) desnames <- paste("X", 1:6, sep="")   # Six X's related to 6 cycles  out <- haplo_surv_discrete(X=X, y="y", time.name="time",          Haplos=Haplos, desnames=desnames, designfunc=designftypes)  names(out$coef) <- c(desnames, types) out$coef #>          X1          X2          X3          X4          X5          X6  #> -1.82153345 -0.61608261 -0.17143057 -1.27152045 -0.28635976 -0.19349091  #> DCGCGCTCACG DTCCGCTGACG ITCAGTTGACG ITCCGCTGAGG  #>  0.79753613  0.65747412  0.06119231  0.31666905  summary(out) #>             Estimate Std.Err     2.5%   97.5%   P-value #> X1          -1.82153  0.1619 -2.13892 -1.5041 2.355e-29 #> X2          -0.61608  0.1895 -0.98748 -0.2447 1.149e-03 #> X3          -0.17143  0.1799 -0.52398  0.1811 3.406e-01 #> X4          -1.27152  0.2631 -1.78719 -0.7559 1.346e-06 #> X5          -0.28636  0.2030 -0.68425  0.1115 1.584e-01 #> X6          -0.19349  0.2134 -0.61184  0.2249 3.647e-01 #> DCGCGCTCACG  0.79754  0.1494  0.50465  1.0904 9.445e-08 #> DTCCGCTGACG  0.65747  0.1621  0.33971  0.9752 5.007e-05 #> ITCAGTTGACG  0.06119  0.2145 -0.35931  0.4817 7.755e-01 #> ITCCGCTGAGG  0.31667  0.1361  0.04989  0.5834 1.999e-02

what: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: Fits cumulative odds model discrete time--event data, handling interval censoring event time known lie within interval \((t_l, t_r]\). model assumes: $$ \text{logit}(P(T \leq t | x)) = \log(G(t)) + x \beta $$ \(G(t)\) baseline cumulative odds function \(\beta\) regression coefficients. equivalent : $$ P(T \leq t | x) = \frac{G(t) \exp(x \beta)}{1 + G(t) \exp(x \beta)} $$

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
code: interval_logitsurv_discrete(   formula,   data,   beta = NULL,   no.opt = FALSE,   method = "NR",   stderr = TRUE,   weights = NULL,   offsets = NULL,   exp.link = 1,   increment = 1,   ... )

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: formula Formula Interval object (e.g., Interval(entry, time)) left-hand side covariates right. Can include cluster() correlated data. data Data frame containing variables formula. beta Starting values optimization (vector length \(p + k\), \(p\) number covariates \(k\) number time intervals). .opt Logical; TRUE, skips optimization returns estimates based provided beta (useful initialization). method Optimization method: "NR" (Newton-Raphson, default) "nlm". stderr Logical; FALSE, returns coefficient estimates. weights Observation weights (follows ID). offsets Offsets (follows ID). exp.link Logical; TRUE, parameterizes increments \(\exp(\alpha) > 0\). increment Logical; TRUE, uses increments \(dG(t) = \exp(\alpha)\) parameters. ... Additional arguments passed optimizer (lava::NR nlm).

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: object class "cumoddsreg" containing: coef Estimated coefficients (baseline time effects covariate effects). se.coef Standard errors coefficients. var Variance-covariance matrix. iid Influence function (IID) decomposition robust variance estimation. ntimes Number distinct time intervals. utimes Unique time points. ploglik Log-likelihood convergence. gradient, hessian Optimization results. call Original function call.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: baseline \(G(t)\) parameterized cumulative sum exponentials (\(G(t) = \sum \exp(\alpha)\)), ensuring positivity. regression coefficients describe log-odds event occurring time \(t\). likelihood maximized observed intervals: $$ L = \prod_i [ P(T_i > t_{il} | x_i) - P(T_i > t_{ir} | x_i) ] $$ \(t_{il}\) \(t_{ir}\) left right endpoints interval subject \(\). Right-censored intervals \(t_{ir} = \infty\).

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: Scheike, T. H. (2024). Discrete time survival analysis interval censoring. mets package documentation.

title: Discrete Time-to-Event Analysis with Interval Censoring — interval_logitsurv_discrete
text: Thomas Scheike

code: library("timereg") set.seed(1) d <- sim_nordic_random(5000,delayed=TRUE,ptrunc=0.7,       cordz=0.5,cormz=2,lam0=0.3,country=FALSE) d$strata <- as.numeric(d$country)+(d$zyg=="MZ")*4 times <- seq(60,100,by=10) ## c1 <- timereg::comp.risk(Event(time,cause)~1+cluster(id),data=d,cause=1, ##   model="fg",times=times,max.clust=NULL,n.sim=0) ## mm=model.matrix(~-1+zyg,data=d) ## out1<-random_cif(c1,data=d,cause1=1,cause2=1,same.cens=TRUE,theta.des=mm) ## summary(out1) ## pc1 <- predict(c1,X=1,se=0) ## plot(pc1) ##  ## dl <- d[!d$truncated,] ## dl <- ipw2(dl,cluster="id",same.cens=TRUE,time="time",entrytime="entry",cause="cause", ##            strata="strata",prec.factor=100) ## cl <- timereg::comp.risk(Event(time,cause)~+1+ ##     cluster(id), ##      data=dl,cause=1,model="fg", ##     weights=dl$indi.weights,cens.weights=rep(1,nrow(dl)), ##           times=times,max.clust=NULL,n.sim=0) ## pcl <- predict(cl,X=1,se=0) ## lines(pcl$time,pcl$P1,col=2) ## mm=model.matrix(~-1+factor(zyg),data=dl) ## out2<-random_cif(cl,data=dl,cause1=1,cause2=1,theta.des=mm, ##                  weights=dl$weights,censoring.weights=rep(1,nrow(dl))) ## summary(out2)

title: Mediation analysis in survival context — mediatorSurv
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Mediation analysis in survival context — mediatorSurv
code: n <- 400 dat <- kumarsimRCT(n,rho1=0.5,rho2=0.5,rct=2,censpar=c(0,0,0,0),           beta = c(-0.67, 0.59, 0.55, 0.25, 0.98, 0.18, 0.45, 0.31),     treatmodel = c(-0.18, 0.56, 0.56, 0.54),restrict=1) dfactor(dat) <- dnr.f~dnr dfactor(dat) <- gp.f~gp drename(dat) <- ttt24~"ttt24*" dat$id <- 1:n dat$ftime <- 1  weightmodel <- fit <- glm(gp.f~dnr.f+preauto+ttt24,data=dat,family=binomial) wdata <- medweight(fit,data=dat)  ### fitting models with and without mediator aaMss2 <- binreg(Event(time,status)~gp+dnr+preauto+ttt24+cluster(id),data=dat,time=50,cause=2) aaMss22 <- binreg(Event(time,status)~dnr+preauto+ttt24+cluster(id),data=dat,time=50,cause=2)  ### estimating direct and indirect effects (under strong strong assumptions)  aaMss <- binreg(Event(time,status)~dnr.f0+dnr.f1+preauto+ttt24+cluster(id),                 data=wdata,time=50,weights=wdata$weights,cause=2) ## to compute standard errors , requires numDeriv ll <- mediatorSurv(aaMss,fit,data=dat,wdata=wdata) summary(ll) #>    n events #>  800    366 #>  #>  400 clusters #> coeffients: #>              Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept) -0.354894  0.163603 -0.675550 -0.034239  0.0301 #> dnr.f01     -0.075193  0.253862 -0.572754  0.422368  0.7671 #> dnr.f11      0.256898  0.082388  0.095421  0.418376  0.0018 #> preauto      0.499033  0.232544  0.043255  0.954812  0.0319 #> ttt24        0.269618  0.261502 -0.242916  0.782153  0.3025 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> (Intercept)  0.70125 0.50888 0.9663 #> dnr.f01      0.92756 0.56397 1.5256 #> dnr.f11      1.29291 1.10012 1.5195 #> preauto      1.64713 1.04420 2.5982 #> ttt24        1.30946 0.78434 2.1862 #>  #>  ## not run bootstrap (to save time) ## bll <- BootmediatorSurv(aaMss,fit,data=dat,k.boot=500)

title: Multinomial Regression Based on phreg — mlogit

title: Multinomial Regression Based on phreg — mlogit
text: ensures \(\sum_j P_j = 1\). model fitted using phreg function expanding data long format strata category. coefficients represent log-Relative-Risk (log-RR) relative baseline group (first level factor, can reset using relevel). Standard errors computed based sandwich form: $$ D U^{-1} \left( \sum U_i^2 \right) D U^{-1} $$ \(U\) score vector \(D\) derivative matrix. Influence functions (possibly robust) can obtained via iid() function. response variable factor. Can also fit cumulative odds model special case interval_logitsurv_discrete.

title: IPTW Cox Regression (Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted) — phreg_IPTW
text: formula Formula phreg. data Data frame risk averaging. treat.model Propensity score model (binary multinomial). treat.var 1/0 variable indicating treatment given (time-dependent weights). weights Optional weights multiply IPTW weights. estpr (=1, default) estimate propensity scores include uncertainty influence function. pi0 Fixed simple weights (estpr=0). se.cluster compute GEE-type standard errors additional cluster structure present. ... Arguments phreg call.

title: IPTW Cox Regression (Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted) — phreg_IPTW
text: object class "phreg" additional IPTW components: IID Influence functions including propensity score uncertainty. iptw IPTW weights used. naive.var Naive variance ignoring propensity score uncertainty.

title: IPTW Cox Regression (Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted) — phreg_IPTW
text: Standard errors computed via influence functions returned IID argument. Propensity scores fitted using either logistic regression (glm) multinomial model (mlogit) two treatment categories. treatment variable must factor identified RHS treat.model. Recurrent events can considered start-stop structure, requiring cluster(id). Robust standard errors computed cases. Time-dependent propensity score weights can computed treat.var used. weight 1 time first (A_0) 2nd treatment (A_1), uses weights $$w_0(A_0) * w_1(A_1)^{t>T_r}$$ $$T_r$$ time 2nd randomization. weights constructed using glm mlogit model based data treat.var=1. propensity score can constructed number treatments similar manner.

title: IPTW Cox Regression (Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted) — phreg_IPTW
text: Thomas Scheike

title: IPTW Cox Regression (Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighted) — phreg_IPTW
code: data <- mets:::simLT(0.7, 100, beta = 0.3, betac = 0, ce = 1, betao = 0.3) dfactor(data) <- Z.f ~ Z out <- phreg_IPTW(Surv(time, status) ~ Z.f, data = data, treat.model = Z.f ~ X) summary(out) #>  #>    n events #>  100     38 #>  #>  100 clusters #> coefficients: #>      Estimate     S.E.  dU^-1/2 P-value #> Z.f1 -0.84702  0.27941  0.23943  0.0024 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>      Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> Z.f1  0.42869 0.24792 0.7413 #>

what: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
text: Efficient implementation Lu-Tsiatis improvement using baseline covariates, extended competing risks recurrent events. results almost equivalent speffSurv function speff2trial package survival case. dynamic censoring augmentation regression also computed gain additional efficiency censoring augmentation. function handles two-stage randomizations recurrent events (start,stop) cluster structure.

title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
code: phreg_rct(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   cens.code = 0,   typesR = c("R0", "R1", "R01"),   typesC = c("C", "dynC"),   weights = NULL,   augmentR0 = NULL,   augmentR1 = NULL,   augmentC = NULL,   treat.model = ~+1,   RCT = TRUE,   treat.var = NULL,   km = TRUE,   level = 0.95,   cens.model = NULL,   estpr = 1,   pi0 = 0.5,   base.augment = FALSE,   return.augmentR0 = FALSE,   mlogit = FALSE,   ... )

title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
text: formula Formula Surv Event outcome (see coxph) treatment variable (randomization 0/1). treatment variable must first covariate right-hand side. data Data frame containing variables referenced formula. cause Numeric code event interest competing risks recurrent events. cens.code Numeric code censoring competing risks recurrent events. typesR Character vector specifying augmentation types randomization (options: "R0" baseline, "R1" post-baseline, "R01" ). typesC Character vector specifying augmentation types censoring (options: "C" static, "dynC" dynamic). weights Weights used phreg. augmentR0 Formula first randomization augmentation (e.g., ~age+sex). augmentR1 Formula second randomization augmentation (e.g., ~age+sex). augmentC Formula censoring augmentation (e.g., ~age+sex). treat.model Propensity score model formula (default ~+1, assuming RCT). RCT Logical; FALSE, uses propensity score adjustment marginal model. treat.var Variable indicating treatment times two-stage randomization. km Logical; use Kaplan-Meier censoring weights (stratified treatment). level Confidence level intervals (default 0.95). cens.model Censoring model formula (default ~strata(treatment)). estpr Numeric code (1/0); estimate propensity scores (default TRUE). pi0 Fixed propensity scores randomizations (estimating). base.augment Logical; covariate augment baselines (R0 augmentation). return.augmentR0 Logical; return augmentation data. mlogit Logical; use multinomial logistic regression propensity scores. ... Additional arguments passed phreg function.

title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
text: object class "phreg_rct" containing: coefs Coefficient estimates treatment effect. iid Influence function (IID) decomposition variance estimation. AugR0, AugR1, AugCdyn, AugClt Augmentation terms different strategies. cumhaz Cumulative hazards. var Variance-covariance matrix. se Standard errors. call Original function call. formula Formula used. data data used (requested). object includes results different augmentation combinations (R0, R1, R01, C, dynC).

title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct
text: method improves efficiency log-rank test utilizing auxiliary baseline covariates reduce variance, particularly useful randomized clinical trials (RCTs) covariate adjustment can increase power.

title: Lu-Tsiatis More Efficient Log-Rank for Randomized Studies with Baseline Covariates — phreg_rct

code: ## Lu, Tsiatis simulation data <- mets:::simLT(0.7,100) dfactor(data) <- Z.f~Z  out <- phreg_rct(Surv(time,status)~Z.f,data=data,augmentR0=~X,augmentC=~factor(Z):X) summary(out) #>                Estimate   Std.Err        2.5%     97.5%    P-value #> Marginal-Z.f1 0.6721162 0.2763128  0.13055314 1.2136793 0.01499718 #> R0_C:Z.f1     0.4990308 0.2230568  0.06184748 0.9362141 0.02527090 #> R0_dynC:Z.f1  0.3950996 0.2172938 -0.03078852 0.8209876 0.06902238 #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.phreg_rct"

title: Weibull-Cox regression — phreg_weibull
code: data(sTRACE, package="mets") sTRACE$entry <- 0 fit1 <- phreg_weibull(Event(entry, time, status == 9) ~ age,              shape.formula = ~age, data = sTRACE) tt <- seq(0,10, length.out=100) pr1 <- predict(fit1, newdata = sTRACE[1, ], times = tt) fit2 <- phreg(Event(time, status == 9) ~ age, data = sTRACE) pr2 <- predict(fit2, newdata = sTRACE[1, ], se = FALSE) if (interactive()) {    plot(pr2$times, pr2$surv, type="s")    lines(tt, pr1[,1,1], col="red", lwd=2) }

what: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
title: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
text: Estimates \(P(N(t) \geq k)\) function time \(t\), range thresholds \(k\), presence terminal event (death). estimator based cumulative incidence "reaching \(k\) events", treating death competing risk. Confidence intervals computed log plain scale.

title: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
code: prob_exceed_recurrent(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   death.code = 2,   cens.code = 0,   exceed = NULL,   marks = NULL,   all.cifs = FALSE,   return.data = FALSE,   conf.type = c("log", "plain"),   level = 0.95,   ... )

title: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
text: formula formula Event response giving exit time status (optionally entry time). right-hand side may include cluster() strata(). data data frame containing variables formula. cause Integer code recurrent event interest. Default 1. death.code Integer code terminal event. Default 2. cens.code Integer code censoring. Default 0. exceed Integer vector thresholds \(k\) evaluate. NULL (default), observed cumulative counts used. marks Optional numeric vector event weights. non-NULL, cumulative counts formed weighted sums events rather simple counts. Must length nrow(data). .cifs Logical. TRUE, fitted cif object threshold returned cif.exceed. Default FALSE. return.data Logical. TRUE, constructed dataset threshold returned dataList. Default FALSE. conf.type Type confidence interval transformation: "log" (default) "plain". level Confidence level. Default 0.95. ... arguments passed cif.

title: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
text: object class "exceed" following components: time Vector evaluation time points. prob Array dimension (length(time), length(exceed) + 1,     nstrata) containing \(P(N(t) \geq k)\) threshold stratum.     first column gives \(P(N(t) < \text{exceed}[1])\). se.prob Standard errors prob. lower, upper Pointwise confidence interval bounds. meanN Estimated mean number events \(E(N(t))\) (single stratum     ; NULL stratified analyses). meanN2, varN Second moment variance \(N(t)\) (single     stratum ). exceed Thresholds evaluated (excluding zero). cif.exceed List fitted cif objects (    .cifs = TRUE). dataList List datasets threshold (    return.data = TRUE). nstrata, strata.levels, strata.name Stratification information. Use plot() summary() methods visualisation   tabulation.

title: Estimate the probability of exceeding k recurrent events by time t — prob_exceed_recurrent
text: threshold \(k\) exceed, function identifies first time subject reaches \(k\) events, fits competing risks model (cif) "reaching \(k\) events" event interest death competing event. Strata supported. marks NULL, event contributes equally; otherwise events weighted mark values cumulative counts formed.

title: Random effects model for competing risks data — random_cif
text: Fits random effects  model describing dependence cumulative incidence curves subjects within cluster.  Given gamma distributed random effects assumed cumulative incidence curves indpendent, marginal cumulative incidence curves form $$ P(T \leq t, cause=1 | x,z) = P_1(t,x,z) = 1- exp( -x^T (t) exp(z^T \beta)) $$ allow regression structure random effects variances may depend cluster covariates.

title: Simulation of Piecewise Constant Hazard Model (Cox) — rchaz
text: piecewise linear cumulative hazard, inverse easy compute. delayed entry \(x\), compute: $$\Lambda^{-1}(\Lambda(x) + E/RR)$$ \(RR\) relative risks \(E\) exponential mean 1. quantity survival function: $$P(T > t | T>x) = \exp(-RR (\Lambda(t) - \Lambda(x)))$$

title: Simulation of Piecewise Constant Hazard Model (Cox) — rchaz

title: Simulation of Piecewise constant hazard models with two causes (Cox). — rcrisk
code: data(bmt);  n <- 100 cox1 <- phreg(Surv(time,cause==1)~tcell+platelet,data=bmt) cox2 <- phreg(Surv(time,cause==2)~tcell+platelet,data=bmt)  X1 <- bmt[,c("tcell","platelet")] xid <- sample(1:nrow(X1),n,replace=TRUE) Z1 <- X1[xid,] Z2 <- X1[xid,] rr1 <- exp(as.matrix(Z1) %*% cox1$coef) rr2 <- exp(as.matrix(Z2) %*% cox2$coef)  d <-  rcrisk(cox1$cum,cox2$cum,rr1,rr2,cens=2/70) dd <- cbind(d,Z1)  d <-  rcrisk(cox1$cum,cox2$cum,rr1,rr2,cens=cbind(c(1,30,68),c(.01,1,3))) dd <- cbind(d,Z1)  par(mfrow=c(1,3)) scox0 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==0)~tcell+platelet,data=dd) plot(scox0); lines(cbind(c(1,30,68),c(.01,1,3)),col=2) ## scox1 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==1)~tcell+platelet,data=dd) scox2 <- phreg(Surv(time,status==2)~tcell+platelet,data=dd) plot(cox1); plot(scox1,add=TRUE,col=2) plot(cox2); plot(scox2,add=TRUE,col=2)  cbind(cox1$coef,scox1$coef,cox2$coef,scox2$coef) #>                [,1]       [,2]       [,3]      [,4] #> tcell    -0.4232606 -0.6578676  0.3991068 0.6638699 #> platelet -0.5654438 -0.1569024 -0.2461474 0.1614852  # 3 causes and censoring  d3 <-  rcrisk(list(cox1$cum,cox2$cum,cox1$cum),NULL,n=100,cens=cbind(c(1,30,68),c(.01,1,3))) dtable(d3,~status) #>  #> status #>  0  1  2  3  #> 25 34 12 29  #>

what: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
text: Fits Ghosh-Lin IPCW (Inverse Probability Censoring Weighted) Cox-type model recurrent events presence terminal event (e.g., death).

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
code: recreg(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   death.code = 2,   cens.code = 0,   cens.model = ~1,   weights = NULL,   offset = NULL,   Gc = NULL,   wcomp = NULL,   marks = NULL,   augmentation.type = c("lindyn.augment", "lin.augment"),   ... )

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
text: formula Formula 'Event' outcome. data Data frame containing variables. cause Cause interest (default 1). death.code Codes terminal event/death (default 2). cens.code Code censoring (default 0). cens.model Formula stratified Cox model without covariates used estimate censoring probabilities. weights Weights score equations. offset Offsets model. Gc Censoring weights time argument. NULL, calculated using Kaplan-Meier estimator (provide \(G_c(T_i-)\)). wcomp Weights composite outcomes (e.g., cause=c(1,3), wcomp might c(1,2)). marks mark value vector data frame, specifying mark value events. augmentation.type Type augmentation augmentation model given (options: "lindyn.augment", "lin.augment"). ... Additional arguments passed lower-level functions.

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
text: object class "recreg" (extending "phreg") containing: coef Estimated coefficients. var Robust variance-covariance matrix. iid Influence functions coefficients. cumhaz Cumulative hazard estimates. se.cumhaz Standard errors cumulative hazard.

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
text: Cox-type model, expectation modeled : $$ E(dN_1(t)|X) = \mu_0(t) dt \exp(X^T \beta) $$ solving Cox-type IPCW weighted score equations: $$ \int (Z - E(t)) w(t) dN_1(t) $$ $$w(t) = G(t) ((T_i \wedge t < C_i)/G_c(T_i \wedge t))$$, $$E(t) = S_1(t)/S_0(t)$$, $$S_j(t) = \sum X_i^j w_i(t) \exp(X_i^T \beta)$$. IID decomposition beta coefficients takes form: $$ \int (Z - E) w(t) dM_1 + \int q(s)/p(s) dM_c $$ returned iid component. Events, deaths, censorings specified via start-stop structure Event call. function identifies via status vector cause codes, censoring codes (cens.code), death codes (death.code). See examples vignettes details.

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Recurrent Events Regression with Terminal Event — recreg
code: ## data with no ties data(hfactioncpx12) hf <- hfactioncpx12 hf$x <- as.numeric(hf$treatment)  dd <- data.frame(treatment=levels(hf$treatment),id=1)  gl <- recreg(Event(entry,time,status)~treatment+cluster(id),data=hf,cause=1,death.code=2) summary(gl) #>  #>     n events #>  2132   1391 #>  #>  741 clusters #> coefficients: #>             Estimate      S.E.   dU^-1/2 P-value #> treatment1 -0.110404  0.078656  0.053776  0.1604 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>            Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> treatment1  0.89547 0.76754 1.0447 #>  head(iid(gl)) #>      treatment1 #> 1 -1.266428e-04 #> 2 -6.112340e-04 #> 3  2.885192e-03 #> 4  1.308207e-03 #> 5  5.404664e-05 #> 6  2.229380e-03 pgl <- predict(gl,dd,se=1);  plot(pgl,se=1)   ## censoring stratified after treatment  gls <- recreg(Event(entry,time,status)~treatment+cluster(id),data=hf, cause=1,death.code=2,cens.model=~strata(treatment)) summary(gls) #>  #>     n events #>  2132   1391 #>  #>  741 clusters #> coefficients: #>             Estimate      S.E.   dU^-1/2 P-value #> treatment1 -0.109509  0.078707  0.053777  0.1641 #>  #> exp(coefficients): #>            Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> treatment1  0.89627 0.76815 1.0458 #>   glss <- recreg(Event(entry,time,status)~strata(treatment)+cluster(id),data=hf, cause=1,death.code=2,cens.model=~strata(treatment)) summary(glss) #>  #>     n events #>  2132   1391 #>  plot(glss)   ## IPCW at 2 years  ll2 <- recregIPCW(Event(entry,time,status)~treatment+cluster(id),data=hf, cause=1,death.code=2,time=2,cens.model=~strata(treatment)) summary(ll2) #>    n events #>  741   1052 #>  #>  741 clusters #> coeffients: #>              Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)  0.452257  0.060901  0.332893  0.571621  0.0000 #> treatment1  -0.078348  0.093520 -0.261644  0.104948  0.4022 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate    2.5%  97.5% #> (Intercept)  1.57186 1.39500 1.7711 #> treatment1   0.92464 0.76979 1.1107 #>  #>   ll2i <- recregIPCW(Event(entry,time,status)~-1+treatment+cluster(id),data=hf, cause=1,death.code=2,time=2,cens.model=~strata(treatment)) summary(ll2i) #>    n events #>  741   1052 #>  #>  741 clusters #> coeffients: #>            Estimate  Std.Err     2.5%    97.5% P-value #> treatment0 0.452257 0.060901 0.332893 0.571621       0 #> treatment1 0.373909 0.070972 0.234806 0.513013       0 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>            Estimate   2.5%  97.5% #> treatment0   1.5719 1.3950 1.7711 #> treatment1   1.4534 1.2647 1.6703 #>  #>

what: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
text: Estimates marginal mean number recurrent events time presence competing terminal event (e.g. death), using nonparametric estimator Ghosh Lin (2000). Two proportional hazards models fitted internally—one recurrent event rate one terminal event—combined form estimator $$\mu(t) = \int_0^t S(u-)\,dR(u),$$ \(S(u)\) marginal survival probability baseline covariate level \(dR(u)\) baseline recurrent event rate among survivors. Robust (sandwich) standard errors computed via influence-function approach Ghosh Lin (2000).

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
code: recurrent_marginal(formula, data, cause = 1, ..., death.code = 2, test = FALSE)  recurrentMarginal(formula, data, ...)

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
text: formula formula Event response left-hand side, specifying entry time, exit time, event status. right-hand side may include cluster() identify subjects strata() stratified analysis. cluster() term required. data data frame containing variables formula. cause Integer code(s) recurrent event interest. Default 1. ... arguments passed phreg. death.code Integer code(s) terminal event. Default 2. test Logical. TRUE, logrank-type test comparing strata computed stored attribute result. Default FALSE.

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
text: object class "recurrent" following components: mu Estimated marginal mean \(\mu(t)\) jump time. se.mu Robust standard error mu. times Jump times estimates computed. St Marginal survival estimate \(S(t)\) jump time. cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, mu), suitable plotting. se.cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, se.mu). object carries three attributes: "logrank" (test result   test = TRUE, otherwise NULL), "cause",   "death.code".

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
