title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
text: Cook, R. J. Lawless, J. F. (1997). Marginal analysis recurrent events terminating event. Statistics Medicine, 16, 911–924. Ghosh, D. Lin, D. Y. (2000). Nonparametric analysis recurrent events death. Biometrics, 56, 554–562.

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Marginal mean estimation for recurrent events with a terminal event — recurrent_marginal
code: data(hfactioncpx12) hf <- hfactioncpx12 hf$x <- as.numeric(hf$treatment)  ## Fit nonparametric baseline models for recurrent events and death xr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 1) ~ cluster(id), data = hf) dr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 2) ~ cluster(id), data = hf)  par(mfrow = c(1, 3)) plot(dr, se = TRUE); title(main = "Death") plot(xr, se = TRUE); title(main = "Recurrent events")  ## Compare naive and robust standard errors for the recurrent event rate rxr <- robust_phreg(xr, fixbeta = 1) plot(rxr, se = TRUE, robust = TRUE, add = TRUE, col = 4)  ## Marginal mean via formula interface outN <- recurrent_marginal(Event(entry, time, status) ~ cluster(id),                            data = hf, cause = 1, death.code = 2) plot(outN, se = TRUE, col = 2, add = TRUE) summary(outN, times = 1:5) #> [[1]] #>        new.time      mean         se   CI-2.5% CI-97.5% strata #> 608           1 0.8282358 0.04844543 0.7385251 0.928844      0 #> 1053          2 1.5139493 0.07039884 1.3820710 1.658412      0 #> 1282          3 2.0244982 0.08351867 1.8672476 2.194992      0 #> 1392          4 2.5004732 0.10843166 2.2967320 2.722288      0 #> 1392.1        5 2.5004732 0.10843166 2.2967320 2.722288      0 #>   ## Stratified analysis with logrank test out <- recurrent_marginal(Event(entry, time, status) ~ strata(treatment) + cluster(id),                           data = hf, cause = 1, death.code = 2, test = TRUE) plot(out, se = TRUE, ylab = "Marginal mean", col = 1:2)  attr(out, "logrank") #>    Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5% P-value #> p1    37.98   27.04 -15.01 90.97  0.1601 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [p1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 1.9737, df = 1, p-value = 0.1601 summary(out, times = 1:5) #> [[1]] #>       new.time      mean         se   CI-2.5% CI-97.5% strata #> 325          1 0.8737156 0.06783343 0.7503858 1.017315      0 #> 555          2 1.5718563 0.09572955 1.3949953 1.771140      0 #> 682          3 2.1184963 0.11385721 1.9066915 2.353829      0 #> 748          4 2.6815219 0.15451005 2.3951619 3.002118      0 #> 748.1        5 2.6815219 0.15451005 2.3951619 3.002118      0 #>  #> [[2]] #>       new.time      mean         se   CI-2.5%  CI-97.5% strata #> 284          1 0.7815557 0.06908585 0.6572305 0.9293989      1 #> 499          2 1.4534055 0.10315606 1.2646561 1.6703258      1 #> 601          3 1.9240624 0.12165771 1.6998008 2.1779119      1 #> 645          4 2.3134997 0.14963892 2.0380418 2.6261880      1 #> 645.1        5 2.3134997 0.14963892 2.0380418 2.6261880      1 #>   ## text: standard causal assumptions (Consistency, Ignorability, Positivity), ATE estimated \(E(Y(1) - Y(0))\), \(Y()\) potential outcome treatment \(\). method uses double robust estimating equations IPCW-adjusted censoring. first covariate formula must treatment effect (factor). factor two levels, multinomial logistic regression (mlogit) used propensity score modeling.

what: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
text: Provides fast implementation Inverse Probability Censoring Weighting (IPCW) regression single time point. fits model: $$ E( \min(T, t) | X ) = \exp( X^T \beta) $$ , case competing risks data: $$ E( (\epsilon=1) (t - \min(T, t)) | X ) = \exp( X^T \beta) $$ represents "Years Lost Due Cause" (RMTL).

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
code: resmeanIPCW(formula, data, outcome = c("rmst", "rmtl"), ...)

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
text: formula Formula Event outcome (e.g., Event(time, cause)). data Data frame containing variables. outcome Outcome type: "rmst" (Restricted Mean Survival Time) "rmtl" (Restricted Mean Time Lost). ... Additional arguments passed binreg, time, cause, cens.model, model, type, etc.

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
text: object class "binreg" containing: coef Coefficient estimates. se Standard errors. var Variance-covariance matrix. iid Influence function decomposition. naive.var Variance known censoring model (applicable). time Time point used. outcome Type outcome analyzed.

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
text: method solves binomial regression IPCW response estimating equation: $$ X \left( \frac{\Delta(\min(T,t)) Y}{G_c(\min(T,t))} - \exp( X^T \beta) \right) = 0 $$ \(\Delta(\min(T,t)) = (\min(T,t) \leq C)\) indicator uncensored time interest. status variable binary, outcome assumed \(Y = \min(T,t)\) (RMST). status two levels (competing risks), outcome \(Y = (t - \min(T,t)) (\text{status}=\text{cause})\) (RMTL specific cause). function supports: IPCW Adjustment: Weights inverse censoring survival probability. Augmentation: Can include augmentation term (type="II" "III") improve efficiency     robustness (Double Robust estimation). Variance Estimation: Based influence function, including adjustments     estimation censoring model.

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW
text: Scheike, T. Holst, K. K. (2024). Restricted mean time lost survival competing risks data using mets R. WIP.

title: Restricted IPCW Mean for Censored Survival Data — resmeanIPCW

code: data(bmt); bmt$time <- bmt$time+runif(nrow(bmt))*0.001  # E( min(T;t) | X ) = exp( a+b X) with IPCW estimation  out <- resmeanIPCW(Event(time,cause!=0)~tcell+platelet+age, bmt,                 time=50, cens.model=~strata(platelet), model="exp") summary(out) #>    n events #>  408    245 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coeffients: #>              Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)  3.014368  0.065112  2.886750  3.141986  0.0000 #> tcell        0.106635  0.138266 -0.164362  0.377631  0.4406 #> platelet     0.247037  0.097339  0.056256  0.437819  0.0112 #> age         -0.185916  0.043567 -0.271305 -0.100527  0.0000 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate     2.5%   97.5% #> (Intercept) 20.37621 17.93493 23.1498 #> tcell        1.11253  0.84844  1.4588 #> platelet     1.28023  1.05787  1.5493 #> age          0.83034  0.76238  0.9044 #>  #>   ## Weighted GLM version (RMST) out2 <- logitIPCW(Event(time,cause!=0)~tcell+platelet+age, bmt,             time=50, cens.model=~strata(platelet), model="exp", outcome="rmst") summary(out2) #>    n   events #>  408 7467.644 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coeffients: #>               Estimate    Std.Err       2.5% 0.5006201  0.0450 #> age         -0.1803174  0.0526445 -0.2834988 -0.0771360  0.0006 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate     2.5%   97.5% #> (Intercept) 20.49446 17.69720 23.7339 #> tcell resmeanIPCW(Event(time,cause!=0)~tcell+platelet+age, bmt,                 time=50, cens.model=~strata(platelet), model="exp", outcome="rmtl") summary(outtl) #>    n events #>  408    245 #>  #>  408 clusters #> coeffients: #>              Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)  3.361796  0.047702  3.268301  3.455291  0.0000 #> tcell       -0.085197  0.127470 -0.335034  0.164641  0.5039 #> platelet    -0.239899  0.100767 -0.437399 -0.042399  0.0173 #> age          0.175625  0.044854  0.087713  0.263537  0.0001 #>  #> exp(coeffients): #>             Estimate     2.5% Kaplan-Meier for full censoring model  bmt$int <- with(bmt, strata(tcell, platelet)) out <- resmeanIPCW(Event(time,cause!=0)~-1+int, bmt, time=30,                              cens.model=~strata(platelet, tcell), model="lin") estimate(out) #>                        Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> inttcell=0, platelet=0    13.60  0.8316 11.97 15.23 3.830e-60 #> inttcell=0, platelet=1    18.90  1.2696 16.41 21.39 4.003e-50 #> inttcell=1, platelet=0    16.19  2.4061 11.48 20.91 1.704e-11 #> inttcell=1, platelet=1    17.77  2.4536 12.96 22.58 4.464e-13 out1 <- phreg(Surv(time,cause!=0)~strata(tcell,platelet), data=bmt) rm1 <- resmean_phreg(out1, times=30) summary(rm1) #>                     strata times    rmean  se.rmean    lower    upper #> tcell=0, platelet=0      0    30 13.60291 0.8315434 12.06697 15.33436 #> tcell=0, platelet=1      1    30 18.90123 1.2693318 16.57016 21.56023 #> tcell=1, platelet=0      2    30 16.19125 2.4006087 12.10811 21.65132 #> tcell=1, platelet=1      3    30 17.76607 2.4422072 13.57004 23.25956 #>                     years.lost #> tcell=0, platelet=0   16.39709 #> tcell=0, platelet=1   11.09877 #> tcell=1, platelet=0   13.80875 #> tcell=1, platelet=1   12.23393  ### Years lost regression outl <- resmeanIPCW(Event(time,cause!=0)~-1+int, bmt, time=30, outcome="years-lost",                              cens.model=~strata(platelet, tcell), model="lin") estimate(outl) #>                        Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> inttcell=0, platelet=0    16.40  0.8315 14.767 18.03 1.486e-86 #> inttcell=0, platelet=1    11.10  1.2693  8.611 13.59 2.254e-18 #> inttcell=1, platelet=0    13.81  2.4006  9.104 18.51 8.810e-09 #> inttcell=1, platelet=1    12.23  2.4423  7.447 17.02 5.467e-07  ## Competing risks years-lost for cause 1   out <- resmeanIPCW(Event(time,cause)~-1+int, bmt, time=30, cause=1,                             cens.model=~strata(platelet, tcell), model="lin") estimate(out) #>                        Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%   P-value #> inttcell=0, platelet=0   12.105  0.8508 10.438 13.773 6.162e-46 #> inttcell=0, platelet=1    6.884  1.1741  4.583  9.185 4.536e-09 #> inttcell=1, platelet=0    7.261  2.3533  2.648 11.873 2.033e-03 #> inttcell=1, platelet=1    5.780  2.0925  1.679  9.882 5.737e-03 ## Same as integrated cumulative incidence  rmc1 <- cif_yearslost(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell,platelet), data=bmt, times=30) summary(rmc1) #> $estimate #> $estimate$intF_1 #>                     strata times    intF_1 se.intF_1 lower_intF_1 upper_intF_1 #> tcell=0, platelet=0      0    30 12.105150 0.8508117    10.547351    13.893030 #> tcell=0, platelet=1      1    30  6.884207 1.1741044     4.928119     9.616714 #> tcell=1, platelet=0 text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simulation of Output from Cumulative Incidence Regression Model — sim_cif
code: data(bmt) nsim <- 100  ## logit cumulative incidence regression model  cif <- cifreg(Event(time,cause)~platelet+age,data=bmt,cause=1) estimate(cif)   #>          Estimate Std.Err    2.5%    97.5%   P-value #> platelet  -0.5300 0.23329 -0.9872 -0.07274 0.0231012 #> age        0.3553 0.09611  0.1669  0.54363 0.0002187 plot(cif,col=1) simbmt <- sim_cif(cif,nsim,data=bmt) dtable(simbmt,~cause) #>  #> cause #>  0  1  #> 61 39  #>  scif <- cifreg(Event(time,cause)~platelet+age,data=simbmt,cause=1) estimate(scif) #>          Estimate Std.Err     2.5%  97.5% P-value #> platelet  -0.3317  0.4475 -1.20884 0.5454 0.45855 #> age        0.4225  0.2433 -0.05446 0.8994 0.08253 plot(scif,add=TRUE,col=2)   ## Fine-Gray cloglog cumulative incidence regression model  cif <- cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+age,data=bmt,cause=1) estimate(cif)   #> Warning: IC does not have mean zero (max |mean|/rms = 6.1e-05). Using lava.options(check.ic = FALSE) disables the warning globally. #>     Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%   P-value #> age   0.3584 0.07883 0.2039 0.5129 5.471e-06 plot(cif,col=1) simbmt <- sim_cif(cif,nsim,data=bmt) scif <- cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+age,data=simbmt,cause=1) estimate(scif) #>     Estimate Std.Err    2.5%  97.5% P-value #> age   0.1246  0.1446 -0.1588 0.4081  0.3887 plot(scif,add=TRUE,col=2)   ################################################################ #  simulating several causes with specific cumulatives  ################################################################ cif1 <-  cifreg(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+age,data=bmt,cause=1) cif2 <-  cifreg(Event(time,cause)~strata(platelet)+tcell+age,data=bmt,cause=2) cifss <-  list(cif1,cif2) simbmt <- sim_cifs(list(cif1,cif2),nsim,data=bmt,extend=0.005) scif1 <-  cifreg(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+age,data=simbmt,cause=1) scif2 <-  cifreg(Event(time,cause)~strata(platelet)+tcell+age,data=simbmt,cause=2) cbind(cif1$coef,scif1$coef)    #>          [,1]      [,2] #> age 0.4157207 0.5536631 ## can be off due to restriction F1+F2<= 1     cbind(cif2$coef,scif2$coef)    #>              [,1]       [,2] #> tcell  0.68378716  1.2132311 #> age   -0.03484858 -0.4479749      par(mfrow=c(1,2))    ## Cause 1 follows the model     plot(cif1); plot(scif1,add=TRUE,col=1:2,lwd=2) # Cause 2:second cause is modified with restriction to satisfy F1+F2<= 1, so scaled down      plot(cif2); plot(scif2,add=TRUE,col=1:2,lwd=2)

title: Simulation of Illness-Death Model — sim_multistate
code: ######################################## ## getting some rates to mimick  ######################################## data(CPH_HPN_CRBSI) dr <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$terminal base1 <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$crbsi  base4 <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$mechanical dr2 <- scalecumhaz(dr,1.5) cens <- rbind(c(0,0),c(2000,0.5),c(5110,3))  iddata <- sim_multistate(100,base1,base1,dr,dr2,cens=cens) dlist(iddata,.~id|id<3,n=0) #> id: 1 #>   entry     time status rr death from to start     stop #> 1     0 618.1512      3  1     1    1  3     0 618.1512 #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #> id: 2 #>   entry     time status rr death from to start     stop #> 2     0 243.4911      3  1     1    1  3     0 243.4911   ### estimating rates from simulated data   c0 <- phreg(Surv(start,stop,status==0)~+1,iddata) c3 <- phreg(Surv(start,stop,status==3)~+strata(from),iddata) c1 <- phreg(Surv(start,stop,status==1)~+1,subset(iddata,from==2)) c2 <- phreg(Surv(start,stop,status==2)~+1,subset(iddata,from==1)) ### par(mfrow=c(2,3)) plot(c0) lines(cens,col=2)  plot(c3,main="rates 1-> 3 , 2->3") lines(dr,col=1,lwd=2) lines(dr2,col=2,lwd=2) ### plot(c1,main="rate 1->2") lines(base1,lwd=2) ### plot(c2,main="rate 2->1") lines(base1,lwd=2)

title: Simulation of Output from Cox Model — sim_phreg
text: Data frame simulated event times, status, covariates.

title: Simulation of Output from Cox Model — sim_phreg
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simulation of Output from Cox Model — sim_phreg
code: data(sTRACE) nsim <- 100 coxs <-  phreg(Surv(time,status==9)~strata(chf)+vf+wmi,data=sTRACE) set.seed(100) sim3 <- sim_phreg(coxs,nsim,data=sTRACE) head(sim3) #>            time status chf vf wmi orig.id #> 1  0.0005400588      1   1  1 0.4     202 #> 45 7.8250000000      0   0  0 1.6     358 #> 46 7.8250000000      0   0  0 1.6     112 #> 47 2.3192112736      1   0  0 1.8     499 #> 48 2.8507169261      1   0  0 1.0     473 #> 49 7.8250000000      0   0  0 1.4     206 cc <-   phreg(Surv(time,status)~strata(chf)+vf+wmi,data=sim3) cbind(coxs$coef,cc$coef) #>           [,1]      [,2] #> vf   0.2907750  1.796828 #> wmi -0.8905339 -1.150499 plot(coxs,col=1); plot(cc,add=TRUE,col=2)   Z <- sim3[,c("vf","chf","wmi")] strata <- sim3[,c("chf")] rr <- exp(as.matrix(Z[,-2]) %*% coef(coxs)) sim4 <- sim_phreg(coxs,nsim,data=NULL,rr=rr,strata=strata) sim4 <- cbind(sim4,Z) cc <-   phreg(Surv(time,status)~strata(chf)+vf+wmi,data=sim4) cbind(coxs$coef,cc$coef) #>           [,1]        [,2] #> vf   0.2907750  0.03925669 #> wmi -0.8905339 -1.28816896 plot(coxs,col=1); plot(cc,add=TRUE,col=2)

NULL, terminal event included. r1 Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risks recurrent event. rd Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risks terminal event. rc Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific multipliers exponential censoring rate. ... arguments passed sim_recurrent_list, including dependence, var.z, gap.time, max.recurrent.

what: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
text: Simulates recurrent event data two distinct event types optional terminal event (death), based user-supplied cumulative hazard functions. Dependence processes can introduced via shared correlated gamma-distributed frailties.

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
code: sim_recurrentII(   n,   cumhaz,   cumhaz2,   death.cumhaz = NULL,   r1 = NULL,   r2 = NULL,   rd = NULL,   rc = NULL,   dependence = 0,   var.z = 1,   cor.mat = NULL,   cens = NULL,   gap.time = FALSE,   max.recurrent = 100,   ... )

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
text: n Number subjects simulate. cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving cumulative hazard first type recurrent event. cumhaz2 Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving cumulative hazard second type recurrent event. death.cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving cumulative hazard terminal event. NULL, terminal event simulated follow-ends end cumhaz. r1 Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risk multipliers first event type. r2 Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risk multipliers second event type. rd Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risk multipliers terminal event. rc Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific multipliers exponential censoring rate. dependence Integer specifying frailty structure. One 0 (independence), 1 (shared gamma frailty), 4 (shared frailty recurrent events ). See Details. var.z Variance gamma-distributed frailty. Default 1. cor.mat Correlation matrix random effects. Used dependence = 2 (sim_recurrent_list). cens Rate exponential censoring. NULL (default), additional censoring applied. gap.time Logical. TRUE, event times drawn gap times (time since last event) rather calendar times. Default FALSE. max.recurrent Maximum number recurrent events allowed per subject. Default 100. ... arguments passed sim_recurrent_list.

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
text: data frame counting-process format (one row per event interval   per subject) columns: id Subject identifier. start, entry Interval start time. stop, time Interval end time (event censoring time). status Event type stop: 1 2     recurrent event corresponding type, 0 censoring. death Indicator terminal event (1) censoring/survival     (0). Attributes "cumhaz", "death.cumhaz", "rr",   "rd" store inputs used simulation.

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
text: simulation proceeds sequentially drawing next event time specified cumulative hazards, taking minimum two recurrent event times, stopping subject death administrative censoring. Dependence processes controlled dependence: 0 Independence: subjects frailty fixed 1. 1 Shared frailty: processes share single     gamma-distributed random effect mean 1 variance var.z. 4 Recurrent-event frailty : two recurrent event     processes share gamma frailty terminal event independent. complex correlation structures across two event types death, use sim_recurrentTS.

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simulate recurrent events with two event types and a terminal event — sim_recurrentII

what: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
text: Simulates recurrent event data two event types terminal event, using parametric two-stage frailty model. construction ensures marginal rates approximately correct: conditional survival, \(E(dN_j \mid D > t) \approx\) cumhazj, hazard death equals death.cumhaz.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
code: sim_recurrentTS(   n,   cumhaz,   cumhaz2,   death.cumhaz = NULL,   nu = rep(1, 3),   share1 = 0.3,   vargamD = 2,   vargam12 = 0.5,   gap.time = FALSE,   max.recurrent = 100,   cens = NULL,   ... )

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
text:
n Number subjects simulate. cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving target marginal cumulative rate first recurrent event type. cumhaz2 Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving target marginal cumulative rate second recurrent event type. death.cumhaz Two-column matrix (time, cumhaz) giving cumulative hazard terminal event. nu Numeric vector length 3: powers \((
u_1, 
u_2, 
u_3)\) applied frailty components (see Details). Must satisfy \(
u_j > -1/	ext{shape}\). Default rep(1, 3). share1 Proportion total death frailty variance assigned first component \(Z_{d1}\). remainder goes \(Z_{d2}\). Must \((0, 1)\). Default 0.3. vargamD Total variance death frailty \(Z_	ext{death}\). Default 2. vargam12 Variance shared recurrent-event frailty \(Z_{12}\). Default 0.5. gap.time Logical. TRUE, event times drawn gap times rather calendar times. Default FALSE. max.recurrent Maximum number recurrent events per subject. Default 100. cens Rate exponential censoring. NULL (default), administrative censoring applied. ... arguments passed lower-level simulation functions.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
text: data frame counting-process format (one row per event interval   per subject) columns id, start, stop,   entry, time, status, death. Attributes   store (possibly adjusted) cumulative hazards used simulation   frailty parameters.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage model with structured gamma frailties — sim_recurrentTS
code: data(CPH_HPN_CRBSI) dr    <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$terminal base1 <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$crbsi base4 <- CPH_HPN_CRBSI$mechanical  rr <- sim_recurrentTS(1000, base1, base4, death.cumhaz = dr) dtable(rr, ~death + status) #>  #>       status    0    1    2 #> death                       #> 0             138 2394  384 #> 1             861    0    0 mets:::showfitsim(causes = 2, rr, dr, base1, base4)

what: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: Simulates recurrent event data fitted two-stage model, recurrent event process terminal event described separate fitted model. recurrent event model may either Cox proportional hazards model (phreg) Ghosh-Lin marginal rate model (recreg); terminal event model must Cox model (phreg).

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
code: sim_recurrent_ts(   cox1,   coxd = NULL,   n = 1,   data = NULL,   type = c("default", "cox-cox", "gl-cox"),   id = "id",   varz = 1,   share = 1,   cens = 0.001,   scale1 = 1,   scaled = 1,   dependence = NULL,   r1 = NULL,   rd = NULL,   rc = NULL,   strata1 = NULL,   stratad = NULL,   death.code = 3,   ... )

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: cox1 fitted phreg object recurrent event rate, fitted recreg (Ghosh-Lin) object. model type detected automatically class. coxd fitted phreg object terminal event. May NULL terminal event modelled. n Number subjects simulate. Default 1. data data frame cox1 coxd fitted, used draw covariate values simulated subjects. NULL, covariates must supplied via r1, rd, strata1, stratad. type Simulation type: "default" (auto-detected class cox1), "cox-cox", "gl-cox". id Name subject identifier variable data. Default "id". varz Variance frailty distribution two-stage model. Default 1. share Proportion shared frailty assigned recurrent event process partial-sharing model. Default 1. cens Rate exponential censoring. Default 0.001. scale1 Scalar multiplier baseline cumulative hazard recurrent event process. Default 1. scaled Scalar multiplier baseline cumulative hazard terminal event. Default 1. dependence non-NULL, falls back sim_recurrent_list frailty structure (see sim_recurrentII valid values). Default NULL. r1 Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risks recurrent event, used data = NULL. rd Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific relative risks terminal event, used data = NULL. rc Optional numeric vector length n subject-specific censoring rate multipliers. strata1 Optional integer vector length n specifying stratum index (0-based) recurrent event model, used data = NULL. stratad Optional integer vector length n specifying stratum index (0-based) terminal event model, used data = NULL. death.code Integer status code used terminal event output status column. Default 3. ... arguments passed sim_GLcox, including nmin nmax linear approximation grid.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: data frame counting-process format one row per event   interval per subject. Column names match original model   formula (entry, exit, status variables). Additional columns include   id covariates drawn data (supplied).   terminal event coded death.code status variable;   recurrent events coded 1.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: Covariates drawn bootstrap data (supplied), subject-specific relative risks strata derived fitted model objects. Stratified baselines fully supported. dependence NULL (default), simulation uses two-stage structure sim_GLcox; setting dependence integer falls back sim_recurrent_list corresponding frailty model.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: Scheike, T. H. (2026). Two-stage recurrent events random effects models. Lifetime Data Analysis.

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Simulate recurrent events from a two-stage Cox or Ghosh-Lin model — sim_recurrent_ts
code: data(hfactioncpx12) hf    <- hfactioncpx12 hf$x  <- as.numeric(hf$treatment) n     <- 100  ## Cox-Cox two-stage model xr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 1) ~ x + cluster(id), data = hf) dr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 2) ~ x + cluster(id), data = hf) simcoxcox <- sim_recurrent_ts(xr, dr, n = n, data = hf, death.code = 2)  ## Ghosh-Lin/Cox two-stage model recGL  <- recreg(Event(entry, time, status) ~ x + cluster(id), hf, death.code = 2) simglcox <- sim_recurrent_ts(recGL, dr, n = n, data = hf, death.code = 2)

text: Thomas Scheike

title: G-Estimator for Cox and Fine-Gray Models — survivalG
code: data(bmt) bmt$time <- bmt$time + runif(408) * 0.001 bmt$event <- (bmt$cause != 0) * 1 bmt$id <- 1:408 dfactor(bmt) <- tcell.f ~ tcell  # Fine-Gray model fg1 <- cifreg(Event(time, cause) ~ tcell.f + platelet + age, bmt,               cause = 1, cox.prep = TRUE, propodds = NULL) summary(survivalG(fg1, bmt, 50)) #> G-estimator : #>       Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%   P-value #> risk0   0.4332 0.02750 0.3793 0.4871 6.349e-56 #> risk1   0.2726 0.05861 0.1577 0.3875 3.297e-06 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: difference (G-estimator) : #>     Estimate Std.Err   2.5%    97.5% P-value #> ps0  -0.1606  0.0635 -0.285 -0.03611 0.01145 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>       Estimate   Std.Err       2.5%       97.5%    P-value #> ps0 -0.4631328 0.2211563 -0.8965913 -0.02967436 0.03624731 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 0.6293090 0.4079579 0.9707616  #>   # Cox model ss <- phreg(Surv(time, event) ~ tcell.f + platelet + age, bmt) summary(survivalG(ss, bmt, 50)) #> G-estimator : #>       Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%    P-value #> risk0   0.6539 0.02708 0.6008 0.7070 8.771e-129 #> risk1   0.5639 0.05971 0.4469 0.6809  3.579e-21 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: difference (G-estimator) : #>     Estimate Std.Err    2.5%  97.5% P-value #> ps0    -0.09 0.06291 -0.2133 0.0333  0.1525 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>       Estimate   Std.Err       2.5%      97.5%   P-value #> ps0 -0.1480738 0.1095493 -0.3627866 0.06663895 0.1764831 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 0.8623675 0.6957349 1.0689095  #>  #> Average Treatment effect:  survival-difference (G-estimator) : #>       Estimate    Std.Err        2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 0.08999732 0.06290582 -0.03329583 0.2132905 0.1525255 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: 1-G (survival)-ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>      Estimate   Std.Err        2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 0.2311351 0.1503551 -0.06355556 0.5258258 0.1242294 #> ratio:  #> Estimate     2.5%    97.5%  #> 1.260029 0.938422 1.691855  #>   # Stratified Cox model ss <- phreg(Surv(time, event) ~ strata(tcell.f) + platelet + age, bmt) summary(survivalG(ss, bmt, 50)) #> G-estimator : #>       Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%    P-value #> risk0   0.6441 0.02727 0.5906 0.6975 2.434e-123 #> risk1   0.6172 0.07125 0.4775 0.7568  4.635e-18 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: difference (G-estimator) : #>     Estimate Std.Err    2.5%  97.5% P-value #> ps0 -0.02693 0.07622 -0.1763 0.1225  0.7238 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>        Estimate   Std.Err       2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 -0.04271539 0.1228586 -0.2835139 0.1980831 0.7280812 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 0.9581841 0.7531327 1.2190637  #>  #> Average Treatment effect:  survival-difference (G-estimator) : #>       Estimate    Std.Err      2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 0.02693334 0.07622097 -0.122457 0.1763237 0.7238196 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: 1-G (survival)-ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>       Estimate   Std.Err      2.5%    97.5%  P-value #> ps0 0.07294849 0.2010713 -0.321144 0.467041 0.716755 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 1.0756751 0.7253188 1.5952668  #>   # Time-varying G-estimates sst <- survivalGtime(ss, bmt, n = 50) #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced plot(sst)   # Among treated (specify id to link influence functions) ss <- phreg(Surv(time, event) ~ tcell.f + platelet + age + cluster(id), bmt) summary(survivalG(ss, subset(bmt, tcell == 1), 50)) #> G-estimator : #>       Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%   P-value #> risk0   0.6663 0.03407 0.5995 0.7331 3.588e-85 #> risk1   0.5749 0.05748 0.4622 0.6875 1.511e-23 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: difference (G-estimator) : #>     Estimate Std.Err    2.5%   97.5% P-value #> ps0 -0.09142 0.06416 -0.2172 0.03434  0.1542 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>      Estimate   Std.Err       2.5%      97.5%   P-value #> ps0 -0.147582 0.1081855 -0.3596217 0.06445766 0.1725181 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 0.8627917 0.6979403 1.0665804  #>  #> Average Treatment effect:  survival-difference (G-estimator) : #>       Estimate    Std.Err       2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 0.09142262 0.06416389 -0.0343363 0.2171815 0.1542064 #>  #> Average Treatment effect: 1-G (survival)-ratio (G-estimator) : #> log-ratio:  #>      Estimate   Std.Err       2.5%     97.5%   P-value #> ps0 0.2421385 0.1620315 -0.0754374 0.5597144 0.1350733 #> ratio:  #>  Estimate      2.5%     97.5%  #> 1.2739707 0.9273378 1.7501727  #>

what: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
text: Fits Clayton-Oakes bivariate Plackett models bivariate survival data using marginals Cox form. dependence can modelled via: Regression design dependence parameter. Random effects, additive gamma model.

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
code: survival_twostage(   margsurv,   data = NULL,   method = "nr",   detail = 0,   clusters = NULL,   silent = 1,   weights = NULL,   theta = NULL,   theta.des = NULL,   var.link = 1,   baseline.iid = 1,   model = "clayton.oakes",   marginal.trunc = NULL,   marginal.survival = NULL,   strata = NULL,   se.clusters = NULL,   numDeriv = 1,   random.design = NULL,   pairs = NULL,   dim.theta = NULL,   numDeriv.method = "simple",   additive.gamma.sum = NULL,   var.par = 1,   no.opt = FALSE,   ... )

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
text: margsurv Marginal model object. data Data frame (must given). method Scoring method: "nr" lava NR optimizer. detail Detail level output. clusters Cluster variable. silent Debug information level. weights Weights score equations. theta Starting values variance components. theta.des Design dependence parameters; pairs given, indices theta-design pair given pairs column 5. var.link Link function variance: 1 exponential link. baseline.iid adjust baseline estimation, using phreg function data. model Model type: "clayton.oakes" "plackett". marginal.trunc Marginal left truncation probabilities. marginal.survival Optional vector marginal survival probabilities. strata Strata fitting (see examples). se.clusters Clusters SE calculation IID. numDeriv get numDeriv version second derivative; otherwise uses sum squared scores pair. random.design Random effect design additive gamma model; pairs given, indices pairs' random.design rows given columns 3:4. pairs Matrix rows indices (two-columns) pairs considered pairwise composite score; useful case-control sampling marginal known. dim.theta Dimension theta parameter pairs situation. numDeriv.method Method numerical derivative (e.g., "simple" speed things). additive.gamma.sum two.stage=0, specification lamtot models via matrix multiplied onto parameters theta (dimensions = number random effects \(\times\) number theta parameters); NULL, sums parameters. var.par 1 default parametrization variances random effects; var.par=0 specifies \(\lambda_j\)'s used parameters. .opt optimizing. ... Additional arguments maximizer NR lava.

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
text: object class "mets.twostage" containing: theta Estimated dependence parameters. coef Coefficients. score Score vector. hess Hessian matrix. hessi Inverse Hessian matrix. var.theta Variance theta parameters. robvar.theta Robust variance theta parameters. loglike Log-likelihood value. theta.iid Influence functions theta. model Model type used.

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
text: clusters contain two subjects, composite likelihood based pairwise bivariate models used (full MLE see twostageMLE). two-stage model constructed , given gamma distributed random effects, survival functions assumed independent, marginal survival functions Cox form: $$ P(T > t| x) = S(t|x)= \exp( -\exp(x^T \beta) A_0(t) ) $$ One possibility model variance within clusters via regression design, specifying regression structure independent gamma distributed random effect cluster, variance given : $$ \theta = h( z_j^T \alpha) $$ \(z\) specified theta.des, possible link function var.link=1 use exponential link \(h(x)=\exp(x)\), var.link=0 identity link \(h(x)=x\). reported standard errors based estimated information likelihood assuming marginals known (unlike twostageMLE additive gamma model ). Can also fit structured additive gamma random effects model, ACE, ADE model survival data. case, random.design specifies random effects subject within cluster. matrix 1's 0's dimension \(n \times d\) (\(d\) random effects). cluster two subjects, random.design rows \(v_1\) \(v_2\). random effect subject 1 $$v_1^T (Z_1,...,Z_d)$$, \(d\) random effects. random effect associated parameter \((\lambda_1,...,\lambda_d)\). construction, subject 1's random effect Gamma distributed mean \(\lambda_j/v_1^T \lambda\) variance \(\lambda_j/(v_1^T \lambda)^2\). Note random effect \(v_1^T (Z_1,...,Z_d)\) mean 1 variance \(1/(v_1^T \lambda)\). assumed \(lamtot=v_1^T \lambda\) fixed within clusters ACE model. Based parameters, relative contribution (heritability, \(h\)) equivalent expected values random effects: \(\lambda_j/v_1^T \lambda\). DEFAULT parametrization (var.par=1) uses variances random effects: $$ \theta_j  = \lambda_j/(v_1^T \lambda)^2 $$ alternative parametrizations, specify parameters relate \(\lambda_j\) argument var.par=0. types models, basic model assumptions given random effects clusters, survival distributions within cluster independent form: $$ P(T > t| x,z) = \exp( -Z \cdot \text{Laplace}^{-1}(lamtot,lamtot,S(t|x)) ) $$ inverse Laplace gamma distribution mean 1 variance \(1/lamtot\). parameters \((\lambda_1,...,\lambda_d)\) related parameters model regression construction pard (\(d \times k\)), links \(d\) \(\lambda\) parameters \(k\) underlying \(\theta\) parameters: $$ \lambda = theta.des \times \theta $$ using theta.des specify low-dimension associations. Default diagonal matrix. can used make structural assumptions variances random-effects needed ACE model example. case.control option can used pair specification pairwise parts estimating equations. assumed second subject pair proband.

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage

title: Twostage Survival Model for Multivariate Survival Data — survival_twostage
code: data(diabetes)  # Marginal Cox model  with treat as covariate margph <- phreg(Surv(time,status)~treat+cluster(id),data=diabetes) ### Clayton-Oakes, MLE fitco1<-twostageMLE(margph,data=diabetes,theta=1.0) summary(fitco1) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> $estimates #>                 Coef.        SE       z       P-val Kendall tau         SE #> dependence1 0.9526614 0.3543033 2.68883 0.007170289    0.322645 0.08127892 #>  #> $type #> NULL #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"  ### Plackett model mph <- phreg(Surv(time,status)~treat+cluster(id),data=diabetes) fitp <- survival_twostage(mph,data=diabetes,theta=3.0,Nit=40,                clusters=diabetes$id,var.link=1,model="plackett") summary(fitp) #> Dependence parameter for Odds-Ratio (Plackett) model  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>             log-Coef.        SE        z        P-val Spearman Corr.         SE #> dependence1   1.14188 0.3026537 3.772891 0.0001613666      0.3648216 0.08869229 #>  #> $or #>             Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> dependence1    3.133  0.9481 1.274 4.991 0.0009528 #>  #> $type #> [1] "plackett" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"  ### Clayton-Oakes fitco2 <- survival_twostage(mph,data=diabetes,theta=0.0,detail=0,                  clusters=diabetes$id,var.link=1,model="clayton.oakes") summary(fitco2) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>              log-Coef.        SE          z     P-val Kendall tau         SE #> dependence1 -0.0484957 0.3718487 -0.1304178 0.8962359    0.322645 0.08126576 #>  #> $vargam #>             Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5%  P-value #> dependence1   0.9527  0.3542 0.2584 1.647 0.007161 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage" fitco3 <- survival_twostage(margph,data=diabetes,theta=1.0,detail=0,              clusters=diabetes$id,var.link=0,model="clayton.oakes") summary(fitco3) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> $estimates #>                 Coef.     SE        z       P-val Kendall tau         SE #> dependence1 0.9526614 0.3543 2.688855 0.007169754    0.322645 0.08127816 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"  ### without covariates but with stratafied marg <- phreg(Surv(time,status)~+strata(treat)+cluster(id),data=diabetes) fitpa <- survival_twostage(marg,data=diabetes,theta=1.0,                 clusters=diabetes$id,model="clayton.oakes") summary(fitpa) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>               log-Coef.        SE          z     P-val Kendall tau         SE #> dependence1 -0.05684062 0.3721207 -0.1527478 0.8785971    0.320824 0.08108359 #>  #> $vargam #>             Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5%  P-value #> dependence1   0.9447  0.3516 0.2557 1.634 0.007203 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"   ### Piecewise constant cross hazards ratio modelling ########################################################  d <- subset(sim_ClaytonOakes(1000,2,0.5,0,stoptime=2,left=0),!truncated) udp <- piecewise_twostage(c(0,0.5,2),data=d,method="optimize",                           id="cluster",timevar="time",                           status="status",model="clayton.oakes",silent=0) #> Data-set  1 out of  4  #>   Number of joint events: 262 of  1000 #> Data-set  2 out of  4  #>   Number of joint events: 136 of  608 #> Data-set  3 out of  4  #>   Number of joint events: 128 of  601 #> Data-set  4 out of  4  #>   Number of joint events: 306 of  471 summary(udp) #> [1] 1 #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model  #> log-coefficient for dependence parameter (SE)  #>          0 - 0.5        0.5 - 2       #> 0 - 0.5  0.593 (0.100)  0.819 (0.125) #> 0.5 - 2  0.578 (0.133)  0.741 (0.079) #>  #> Kendall's tau (SE)  #>          0 - 0.5        0.5 - 2       #> 0 - 0.5  0.475 (0.025)  0.531 (0.031) #> 0.5 - 2  0.471 (0.033)  0.512 (0.020) #>    ## Reduce Ex.Timings ### Same model using the strata option, a bit slower ######################################################## ## makes the survival pieces for different areas in the plane ##ud1=surv_boxarea(c(0,0),c(0.5,0.5),data=d,id="cluster",timevar="time",status="status") ##ud2=surv_boxarea(c(0,0.5),c(0.5,2),data=d,id="cluster",timevar="time",status="status") ##ud3=surv_boxarea(c(0.5,0),c(2,0.5),data=d,id="cluster",timevar="time",status="status") ##ud4=surv_boxarea(c(0.5,0.5),c(2,2),data=d,id="cluster",timevar="time",status="status")  ## everything done in one call ud <- piecewise_data(c(0,0.5,2),data=d,timevar="time",status="status",id="cluster") ud$strata <- factor(ud$strata); ud$intstrata <- factor(ud$intstrata)  ## makes strata specific id variable to identify pairs within strata ## se's computed based on the id variable across strata "cluster" ud$idstrata <- ud$id+(as.numeric(ud$strata)-1)*2000  marg2 <- timereg::aalen(Surv(boxtime,status)~-1+factor(num):factor(intstrata),                data=ud,n.sim=0,robust=0) tdes <- model.matrix(~-1+factor(strata),data=ud) fitp2 <- survival_twostage(marg2,data=ud,se.clusters=ud$cluster,clusters=ud$idstrata,                 model="clayton.oakes",theta.des=tdes,step=0.5) summary(fitp2) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>                           log-Coef.         SE        z        P-val #> factor(strata)0-0.5,0-0.5 0.7677352 0.10124764 7.582747 3.375078e-14 #> factor(strata)0-0.5,0.5-2 0.5719969 0.14361250 3.982919 6.807407e-05 #> factor(strata)0.5-2,0-0.5 0.3717046 0.15778635 2.355746 1.848555e-02 #> factor(strata)0.5-2,0.5-2 0.6915363 0.07753039 8.919552 0.000000e+00 #>                           Kendall tau         SE #> factor(strata)0-0.5,0-0.5   0.5186384 0.02527674 #> factor(strata)0-0.5,0.5-2   0.4697494 0.03577171 #> factor(strata)0.5-2,0-0.5   0.4203242 0.03844492 #> factor(strata)0.5-2,0.5-2   0.4995973 0.01938258 #>  #> $vargam #>             Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> dependence1    2.155  0.2182 1.727 2.582 5.250e-23 #> dependence2    1.772  0.2545 1.273 2.271 3.327e-12 #> dependence3    1.450  0.2288 1.002 1.899 2.332e-10 #> dependence4    1.997  0.1548 1.693 2.300 4.609e-38 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"  ### now fitting the model with symmetry, i.e. strata 2 and 3 same effect ud$stratas <- ud$strata; ud$stratas[ud$strata=="0.5-2,0-0.5"] <- "0-0.5,0.5-2" tdes2 <- model.matrix(~-1+factor(stratas),data=ud) fitp3 <- survival_twostage(marg2,data=ud,clusters=ud$idstrata,se.cluster=ud$cluster,                 model="clayton.oakes",theta.des=tdes2,step=0.5) summary(fitp3) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>                            log-Coef.         SE        z        P-val #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0-0.5 0.7677352 0.10124764 7.582747 3.375078e-14 #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0.5-2 0.4740701 0.12104406 3.916509 8.984052e-05 #> factor(stratas)0.5-2,0.5-2 0.6915363 0.07753039 8.919552 0.000000e+00 #>                            Kendall tau         SE #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0-0.5   0.5186384 0.02527674 #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0.5-2   0.4454487 0.02990081 #> factor(stratas)0.5-2,0.5-2   0.4995973 0.01938258 #>  #> $vargam #>             Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> dependence1    2.155  0.2182 1.727 2.582 5.250e-23 #> dependence2    1.607  0.1945 1.225 1.988 1.439e-16 #> dependence3    1.997  0.1548 1.693 2.300 4.609e-38 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"  ### same model using strata option, a bit slower fitp4 <- survival_twostage(marg2,data=ud,clusters=ud$cluster,se.cluster=ud$cluster,                 model="clayton.oakes",theta.des=tdes2,step=0.5,strata=ud$strata) summary(fitp4) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> With log-link  #> $estimates #>                            log-Coef.         SE        z        P-val #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0-0.5 0.7677352 0.10124764 7.582747 3.375078e-14 #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0.5-2 0.4740701 0.12104406 3.916509 8.984052e-05 #> factor(stratas)0.5-2,0.5-2 0.6915363 0.07753039 8.919552 0.000000e+00 #>                            Kendall tau         SE #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0-0.5   0.5186384 0.02527674 #> factor(stratas)0-0.5,0.5-2   0.4454487 0.02990081 #> factor(stratas)0.5-2,0.5-2   0.4995973 0.01938258 #>  #> $vargam #>             Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> dependence1    2.155  0.2182 1.727 2.582 5.250e-23 #> dependence2    1.607  0.1945 1.225 1.988 1.439e-16 #> dependence3    1.997  0.1548 1.693 2.300 4.609e-38 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"    ## Reduce Ex.Timings ### structured random effects model additive gamma ACE ### simulate structured two-stage additive gamma ACE model data <- sim_ClaytonOakes_twin_ace(2000,2,1,0,3) out <- twin_polygen_design(data,id="cluster") pardes <- out$pardes pardes #>      [,1] [,2] #> [1,]  1.0    0 #> [2,]  0.5    0 #> [3,]  0.5    0 #> [4,]  0.5    0 #> [5,]  0.0    1 des.rv <- out$des.rv head(des.rv) #>   MZ DZ DZns1 DZns2 env #> 1  1  0     0     0   1 #> 2  1  0     0     0   1 #> 3  1  0     0     0   1 #> 4  1  0     0     0   1 #> 5  1  0     0     0   1 #> 6  1  0     0     0   1 aa <- phreg(Surv(time,status)~x+cluster(cluster),data=data,robust=0) ts <- survival_twostage(aa,data=data,clusters=data$cluster,detail=0,          theta=c(2,1),var.link=0,step=0.5,          random.design=des.rv,theta.des=pardes) summary(ts) #> Dependence parameter for Clayton-Oakes model #> Variance of Gamma distributed random effects  #> $estimates #>                 Coef.        SE         z        P-val Kendall tau         SE #> dependence1 2.1349984 0.2124813 10.047936 0.000000e+00   0.5163239 0.02485421 #> dependence2 0.7883763 0.1698435  4.641782 3.454178e-06   0.2827367 0.04368940 #>  #> $type #> [1] "clayton.oakes" #>  #> $h #>      Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5%   P-value #> [1,]   0.7303 0.05871 0.6153 0.8454 1.584e-35 #> [2,]   0.2697 0.05871 0.1546 0.3847 4.355e-06 #>  #> $vare #> NULL #>  #> $vartot #>    Estimate Std.Err  2.5% 97.5%   P-value #> p1    2.923  0.1387 2.652 3.195 1.276e-98 #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "summary.mets.twostage"

what: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: Estimates casewise concordance based concordance marginal estimates, performs tests independence assumption. particularly useful twin studies assess genetic vs. environmental contributions disease risk.

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
code: test_casewise(conc, marg, test = "no-test", p = 0.01)

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: conc object containing concordance estimates (e.g., bicomprisk). Must contain time, P1, se.P1, ideally P1.iid. marg object containing marginal cumulative incidence estimates (e.g., comp.risk prodlim). Must contain time, P1, se.P1, ideally P1.iid. test Type test: "case" (test casewise concordance) "conc" (test concordance). Default "-test". p Threshold checking marginal probability greater value point (default 0.01). Used avoid division near-zero probabilities.

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: object class "casewise" containing: casewise Matrix time, casewise concordance, standard errors. marg Matrix time, marginal CIF, standard errors. conc Matrix time, concordance, standard errors. casewise.iid Influence function decomposition casewise concordance. test Test results (test specified): Pepe-Mori type test statistics,     standard errors, z-values, p-values. mintime, maxtime Time range used analysis. .cluster Logical indicating clusters assumed identical. testtype Type test performed.

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: casewise concordance defined : $$ C(t) = \frac{P(T_1 \leq t, T_2 \leq t)}{P(T_1 \leq t)} $$ numerator joint probability twins event time \(t\), denominator marginal probability. function supports two types tests: "case": Tests casewise concordance scale (difference observed expected independence). "conc": Tests concordance scale (difference observed concordance squared marginal). Standard errors computed using cluster-based conservative estimates influence functions (IID) available input objects.

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: Scheike, T. H. (2024). Casewise concordance estimation testing. mets package documentation.

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Test for Independence Using Casewise Concordance — test_casewise
code: ## Reduce Ex.Timings library("timereg") data("prt",package="mets"); prt <- force_same_cens(prt,cause="status")  prt <- prt[which(prt$id %in% sample(unique(prt$id),7500)),] ### marginal cumulative incidence of prostate cancer times <- seq(60,100,by=2) outm <- timereg::comp.risk(Event(time,status)~+1,data=prt,cause=2,times=times)  cifmz <- predict(outm,X=1,uniform=0,resample.iid=1) cifdz <- predict(outm,X=1,uniform=0,resample.iid=1)  ### concordance for MZ and DZ twins cc <- bicomprisk(Event(time,status)~strata(zyg)+id(id),                  data=prt,cause=c(2,2)) #> Strata 'DZ' #> Strata 'MZ' cdz <- cc$model$"DZ" cmz <- cc$model$"MZ"  ### To compute casewise cluster argument must be passed on, ###  here with a max of 100 to limit comp-time outm <- timereg::comp.risk(Event(time,status)~+1,data=prt,                  cause=2,times=times,max.clust=100) cifmz <- predict(outm,X=1,uniform=0,resample.iid=1) cc <- bicomprisk(Event(time,status)~strata(zyg)+id(id),data=prt,                 cause=c(2,2),se.clusters=outm$clusters) #> Strata 'DZ' #> Strata 'MZ' cdz <- cc$model$"DZ" cmz <- cc$model$"MZ"  cdz <- test_casewise(cdz,cifmz,test="case") ## test based on casewise cmz <- test_casewise(cmz,cifmz,test="conc") ## based on concordance  plot(cmz,ylim=c(0,0.7),xlim=c(60,100)) par(new=TRUE) plot(cdz,ylim=c(0,0.7),xlim=c(60,100))

title: Compare Two Concordance Estimates — test_conc

what: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: Tests whether marginal mean number recurrent events differs across groups (strata), extending classical logrank test setting recurrent events competing terminal event. test statistic $$z = \int_0^\tau w(s)\bigl[d\hat\mu_1(s) - d\hat\mu_2(s)\bigr],$$ \(w(s)\) weight function \(\hat\mu_j(s)\) estimated marginal mean group \(j\). Variance estimated robustly via influence functions Ghosh Lin (2000).

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
code: test_logrankRecurrent(   recurrent,   death,   weight = c("I", "II"),   km = TRUE,   start = 0,   stop = NULL,   at.risk = 5,   cluster.id = NULL,   ... )

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: recurrent Either "recurrent" object returned recurrent_marginal, "phreg" object recurrent event model (case death must also supplied). death "phreg" object terminal event model. Required recurrent "phreg" object; ignored otherwise. weight Character string specifying weight scheme: "", "II", "III". Default "". km Logical. TRUE (default), Kaplan-Meier estimator used survival probability \(S(t)\); otherwise Nelson-Aalen estimator used. start Left truncation time integration. Default 0. stop Right truncation time integration. Defaults last observed jump time. .risk Minimum combined risk-set size weight set zero. Default 5. cluster.id Optional vector cluster identifiers aggregating influence functions across clusters forming test statistic. ... Currently unused.

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: object class "estimate" (lava package)   following components: coef weighted difference marginal means groups. se Robust standard error test statistic. lower, upper 95% confidence interval bounds. p.value Two-sided p-value null hypothesis difference. object also carries iid attribute containing   subject-level influence function decomposition test statistic,   can used inference combination estimators.

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: Three weight schemes available: "" (Default) \(w(t) = R_1(t) R_2(t) / (R_1(t) + R_2(t))\),     \(R_j(t) = Y_j(t) / \hat S_j(t-)\). Analogous standard     logrank weight. "II" \(w(t) = Y_j(t)\), raw risk-set size. Equivalent     using observed counts without survival adjustment. "III" modified weight incorporating cumulative incidence,     analogous Gray's test competing risks.

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: Ghosh, D. Lin, D. Y. (2000). Nonparametric analysis recurrent events death. Biometrics, 56, 554–562.

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Logrank-type test for comparing recurrent event marginal means between groups — test_logrankRecurrent
code: data(hfactioncpx12) hf <- hfactioncpx12  ## Test using two separate phreg models xr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 1) ~ strata(treatment) + cluster(id), data = hf) dr <- phreg(Surv(entry, time, status == 2) ~ strata(treatment) + cluster(id), data = hf) out <- test_logrankRecurrent(xr, dr, stop = 5) summary(out) #> Call: estimate.default(f = FALSE, contrast = contrast, vcov = vcov(object),  #>     coef = p) #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>    Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5% P-value #> p1    37.98   27.04 -15.01 90.97  0.1601 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [p1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 1.9737, df = 1, p-value = 0.1601  ## Equivalently, using a recurrent_marginal object directly outN <- recurrent_marginal(Event(entry, time, status) ~ strata(treatment) + cluster(id),                            data = hf, cause = 1, death.code = 2) test_logrankRecurrent(outN) #>    Estimate Std.Err   2.5% 97.5% P-value #> p1    37.98   27.04 -15.01 90.97  0.1601 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [p1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 1.9737, df = 1, p-value = 0.1601

what: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: Performs score-test type tests proportionality marginal means competing risks data recurrent events, presented Ghosh Lin (2000). test based IPCW (Inverse Probability Censoring Weighting) formulation.

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
code: test_marginalMean(   formula,   data,   cause = 1,   cens.code = 0,   ...,   death.code = 2,   death.code.prop = NULL,   time = NULL,   beta = NULL )

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: formula Formula Event object left-hand side covariates (typically strata() group comparison) right. Can include cluster(id) correlated data. data Data frame containing variables referenced formula. cause Cause interest (default 1). cens.code Censoring code (default 0). ... Additional arguments passed lower-level functions. death.code Code death (terminating event, default 2). death.code.prop Code causes death Fine-Gray regression model. time Upper limit Pepe-Mori AUC integrals. NULL, defaults maximum event time cause interest. beta Starting values score test (default NULL, uses zeros).

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: object class "marginalTest" containing: pepe.mori Pepe-Mori test results compare p-value. RatioAUC Ratio AUC test results compare p-value. difAUC Difference AUC test results compare p-value. prop.test Proportionality test results. score.test Score test results (equivalent Gray's test). score.iid Influence function score test. time Upper time limit used. RAUCl, RAUCe Raw transformed AUC estimates.

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: function computes several tests: Pepe-Mori Test: Tests equality marginal mean functions groups. Ratio AUC: Compares area curve marginal means. Difference AUC: Tests difference areas curve. Score Test: Tests proportionality (equivalent Gray's test CIF). Proportionality Test: Tests proportional hazards assumption. Pepe-Mori test uses weights based number risk group construct weighted integral difference marginal means.

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: Ghosh, D. Lin, D. Y. (2000). Nonparametric Analysis Recurrent Events Death. Biometrics, 56, 554–562.

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Pepe-Mori Test for Marginal Mean Comparison — test_marginalMean
code: data(bmt,package="mets") bmt$time <- bmt$time+runif(nrow(bmt))*0.01 bmt$id <- 1:nrow(bmt) dcut(bmt) <- age.f~age       fg=cifregFG(Event(time,cause)~tcell,data=bmt,cause=1)   ## computing tests for difference  for CIF pmt <- test_marginalMean(Event(time,cause)~strata(tcell)+cluster(id),data=bmt,cause=1,        death.code=1:2,death.code.prop=2,cens.code=0,time=40) #> Warning: IC does not have mean zero (max |mean|/rms = 0.11). Using lava.options(check.ic = FALSE) disables the warning globally. summary(pmt)  #> coeffients: #>                            p-value #> time                       40.0000 #> Pepe-Mori                   0.0203 #> Ratio-AUC                   0.0494 #> Proportionality             0.0500 #> Proportionality-score-test  0.0292 #>    pmt$pepe.mori #>                         Estimate Std.Err   2.5%  97.5% P-value #> factor(strata__)tcell=1   -6.409   2.762 -11.82 -0.996 0.02031 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [factor(strata__)tcell=1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 5.385, df = 1, p-value = 0.02031 pmt$RatioAUC #>             Estimate Std.Err    2.5%     97.5% P-value #> (Intercept)   -0.484  0.2463 -0.9667 -0.001352 0.04936 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [(Intercept)] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 3.863, df = 1, p-value = 0.04936 pmt$prop.test #>                         Estimate Std.Err   2.5%      97.5% P-value #> factor(strata__)tcell=1  -0.5157  0.2631 -1.031 -5.483e-05 0.04998 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [factor(strata__)tcell=1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 3.8423, df = 1, p-value = 0.04998 ## score test equialent to Gray's test but variance estimated differently  pmt$score.test #>    Estimate Std.Err   2.5%   97.5% P-value #> p1   -8.633   3.957 -16.39 -0.8764 0.02915 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Null Hypothesis:  #>   [p1] = 0  #>   #> chisq = 4.7586, df = 1, p-value = 0.02915   ### age-groups   pmt <- test_marginalMean(Event(time,cause)~strata(age.f)+cluster(id),data=bmt,cause=1,        death.code=1:2,death.code.prop=2,cens.code=0) #> Warning: IC does not have mean zero (max |mean|/rms = 0.097). Using lava.options(check.ic = FALSE) disables the warning globally. summary(pmt)  #> coeffients: #>                            p-value #> time                       70.6322 #> Pepe-Mori                       NA #> Ratio-AUC                   0.0060 #> Proportionality             0.0018 #> Proportionality-score-test  0.0002 #>    ## having a look at the cumulative incidences  cifs <- cif(Event(time,cause)~strata(age.f)+cluster(id),data=bmt,cause=1) plot(cifs)     ## recurrent events    data(hfactioncpx12) hf <- hfactioncpx12 pmt <- test_marginalMean(Event(entry,time,status)~strata(treatment)+cluster(id),data=hf,        cause=1,death.code=2,cens.code=0) #> Warning: IC does not have mean zero (max |mean|/rms = 0.051). Using lava.options(check.ic = FALSE) disables the warning globally. summary(pmt)  #> coeffients: #>                            p-value #> time                        3.9794 #> Pepe-Mori                   0.2918 #> Ratio-AUC                   0.2278 #> Proportionality             0.1641 #> Proportionality-score-test  0.1611 #>

what: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
text: Resolves tied event times counting-process dataset adding small random perturbation duplicated exit times event rows. preprocessing step required recurrent_marginal related functions, assume unique jump times within stratum.

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
code: tie_breaker(   data,   stop = "time",   start = "entry",   status = "status",   id = NULL,   cause = NULL,   cens.code = 0,   exit.unique = TRUE,   ddt = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
text: data data frame counting-process format, sorted subject time. stop Name column containing interval exit (stop) times. Default "time". start Name column containing interval entry (start) times, used update following row id supplied. Default "entry". status Name column containing event status codes. Default "status". id Name column containing subject identifiers. supplied, start time next interval subject adjusted match perturbed stop time, preserving interval continuity. Default NULL (adjustment made). cause Integer vector status codes identify events (non-censored rows). NULL (default), non-censoring status values treated events. cens.code Integer code(s) censoring. Rows status never perturbed. Default 0. exit.unique Logical. TRUE (default), event time considered tied whenever coincides exit time data (including censored rows). FALSE, ties among event rows resolved. ddt Maximum perturbation size. Tied event times shifted uniform draw \([0, \text{ddt}]\). NULL (default), ddt set half smallest positive gap two consecutive exit times data. seed Optional integer passed set.seed() drawing perturbations, making results reproducible. Default NULL (seed set).

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
text: input data frame data tied event exit times   perturbed. id supplied, additional logical column   tiebreaker marks rows whose start time adjusted consequence   perturbation preceding row.

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
text: tie defined event exit time (rows status cause code) coincides another exit time dataset. exit.unique = TRUE (default), tie flagged whenever event time also appears among exit times (censored event). exit.unique = FALSE, exact ties two event rows resolved. Tied event times perturbed adding \(U \cdot \delta\) \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\) \(\delta\) ddt (defaulting half smallest observed positive gap consecutive exit times). subject IDs provided via id, corresponding interval start time immediately following row subject updated maintain valid counting-process structure, logical tiebreaker column added flag affected rows.

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
text: Thomas Scheike

title: Break ties in event times for recurrent event data — tie_breaker
code: data(hfactioncpx12) hf <- hfactioncpx12  ## Check for ties in event exit times ev <- hf[hf$status == 1, ] any(duplicated(ev$time)) #> [1] FALSE  ## Resolve ties before fitting the marginal mean model hf_clean <- tie_breaker(hf, stop = "time", start = "entry",                          status = "status", id = "id",                          cause = 1, cens.code = 0)  out <- recurrent_marginal(Event(entry, time, status) ~ cluster(id),                            data = hf_clean, cause = 1, death.code = 2) summary(out, times = 1:5) #> [[1]] #>        new.time      mean         se   CI-2.5% CI-97.5% strata #> 608           1 0.8282358 0.04844543 0.7385251 0.928844      0 #> 1053          2 1.5139493 0.07039884 1.3820710 1.658412      0 #> 1282          3 2.0244982 0.08351867 1.8672476 2.194992      0 #> 1392          4 2.5004732 0.10843166 2.2967320 2.722288      0 #> 1392.1        5 2.5004732 0.10843166 2.2967320 2.722288      0 #>

code: p11_binomial_twostage_RV(   theta,   rv1,   rv2,   p1,   p2,   pardes,   ags = NULL,   link = 0,   i = 1,   j = 1 )  concordanceTwostage(   theta,   p,   rv1,   rv2,   theta.des,   additive.gamma.sum = NULL,   link = 0,   var.par = 0,   ... )  concordanceTwinACE(   object,   rv1 = NULL,   rv2 = NULL,   xmarg = NULL,   type = "ace",   ... )  kendall_ClaytonOakes_twin_ace(parg, parc, K = 10000, test = 0)  kendall.ClaytonOakes.twin.ace(x, y, ...)  kendall_normal_twin_ace(parg, parc, K = 10000)  ascertained_pairs(pairs, data, cr.models, bin = FALSE)  twin.polygen.design(x, ...)  ace_family_design(   data,   id = "id",   member = "type",   mother = "mother",   father = "father",   child = "child",   child1 = "child",   type = "ace",   ... )  make_pairwise_design(pairs, kinship, type = "ace")

title: Concordance Probability from Twostage Model — p11_binomial_twostage_RV
text: theta dependence parameter vector. rv1 random-effects design subject 1. rv2 random-effects design subject 2. p1 marginal probability subject 1. p2 marginal probability subject 2. pardes parameter design matrix. ags additive gamma sum matrix (optional). link link function indicator (0 = identity, 1 = log). index subject 1. j index subject 2. p matrix marginal probabilities (n x 2). theta.des parameter design matrix linking theta lambda parameters. additive.gamma.sum optional matrix additive gamma sums. var.par 1, parameters rescaled sum squared. ... additional arguments. object binomial twostage model object. xmarg optional covariate values marginal probabilities. type model type: "ace", "ade", "ae", "de", "dce", "un". parg genetic variance parameter (gamma shape genetic component). parc common environment variance parameter (gamma shape environment). K number simulated twin pairs (multiplied 2 internally). test 1, prints diagnostic correlations. x passed parg (alias wrapper). y passed parc (alias wrapper). pairs matrix pair indices (n x 2). data data.frame twin/family data. cr.models formula specifying time status variables. bin logical; TRUE uses binary (prevalence) ordering rather time ordering. id character name cluster (pair) identifier column. member character name family member type column. mother value identifying mothers member column. father value identifying fathers member column. child value identifying children member column. child1 column name distinguishing first child second. kinship vector kinship coefficients pair.

title: Concordance Probability from Twostage Model — p11_binomial_twostage_RV
text: list concordance tables, one per pair, containing   pmat (2x2 probability matrix), casewise (casewise concordance),   marg (marginal probabilities). list concordance tables per zygosity group. list components: pardes parameter design matrix linking random effects variance parameters. des.rv random-effects design matrix subjects.

title: Concordance Probability from Twostage Model — p11_binomial_twostage_RV
text: twin_polygen_design creates polygenic random-effects design twin pairs, distinguishing MZ DZ twins. twin.polygen.design alias twin_polygen_design. ace_family_design creates designs nuclear families (mother, father, children). make_pairwise_design creates pairwise random-effects designs arbitrary kinship structures. concordanceTwostage computes concordance probabilities twostage model. concordanceTwinACE computes concordance twin ACE model. kendall_ClaytonOakes_twin_ace kendall_normal_twin_ace compute Kendall's tau Clayton-Oakes normal-frailty twin ACE models respectively. ascertained_pairs identifies ascertained (affected) pairs clustered survival data. p11_binomial_twostage_RV computes joint probability P(T1<=t, T2<=t) additive gamma binary random effects model.
